name: INDEPENDENT REGIONS
problem: Metropolitan areas cannot achieve equilibrium unless they are small enough and self-sufficient enough to function as autonomous regions
solution: Strive to develop autonomous regions worldwide; each containing between 2 and 10 million people; each defined by natural geographic boundaries; each maintaining its own economic system; each self-governing with independent authority; each holding representation in a global governing body, without being controlled by larger national or state entities.

name: THE DISTRIBUTION OF TOWNS
problem: When a region has too many small villages, modern development becomes impossible; conversely, when a region is dominated by large cities, environmental damage occurs because people aren't distributed where they need to be to properly care for the land.
solution:
Foster a natural process for town development within the region that gradually creates:
1. A balanced population distribution across different town sizes - for instance, one town of 1,000,000 people, 10 towns with 100,000 people each, 100 towns with 10,000 people each, and 1,000 towns with 100 people each.
2. A spatial arrangement where towns of similar size are evenly distributed throughout the entire region.
This distribution can be achieved through regional planning policies, land allocation, and economic incentives that guide industry placement according to this distribution model.
towns of 1,000,000 - 250 miles apart
towns of 100,000 - 80 miles apart
towns of 10,000 - 25 miles apart
towns of 1,000 - 8 miles apart

name: CITY COUNTRY FINGERS
problem: Uninterrupted urban expansion damages ecosystems and makes cities unlivable. Yet the scale and density of cities also provides important benefits and opportunities.
solution: Maintain alternating strips of urban development and agricultural land that extend even into metropolitan centers. Urban zones should always be less than 1 mile in width, while agricultural zones must be at least 1 mile wide.

name: AGRICULTURAL VALLEYS
problem: Land with optimal conditions for agriculture is typically also ideal for construction. However, this fertile land is finite - and once developed, its agricultural potential cannot be restored for generations.
solution: Designate all agricultural valleys as protected farmland and prevent any development that would compromise the inherent soil fertility. Maintain this protection even for currently uncultivated valleys: reserve them for future agricultural use, parkland, or natural wilderness.

name: LACE OF COUNTRY STREETS
problem: Suburban development has become an outdated and internally conflicted form of human settlement.
solution: Where urban and rural areas meet, create a network of country roads spaced at least one mile apart, forming squares that enclose countryside or farmland of at least one square mile each. Develop residential properties along these roads, limited to one property deep, with each lot being at least half an acre, and with the enclosed square mile of open land or farmland situated behind the houses.

name: COUNTRY TOWNS
problem: Large cities act as powerful attractors. Small towns struggle to maintain their viability when faced with the gravitational pull of major urban centers.
solution: Maintain existing country towns and support the development of new self-sufficient towns with populations between 500 and 10,000, completely surrounded by open countryside and separated from other towns by at least 10 miles. Make it a regional priority to help each town establish a foundation of local industry, ensuring these communities function as complete towns rather than bedroom communities for commuters - places capable of supporting the full spectrum of human activities.

name: THE COUNTRYSIDE
problem: I conceive that land belongs for use to a vast family of which many are dead, few are living, and countless members are still unborn. - a Nigerian tribesman
solution:
Reclassify all farms as parkland where public access is permitted; similarly, transform all regional parks into working agricultural operations.
Establish stewardship arrangements with families, cooperatives, and other groups, with each stewardship responsible for a specific portion of the countryside. These stewards receive land leases and have authority to manage the land and establish guidelines for its use - whether as farmland, forest, wetland, desert, or other ecosystems. The public maintains the right to visit, hike, picnic, and explore these areas, provided they adhere to the established guidelines. Under this arrangement, farms near urban areas might host picnickers in their fields throughout the summer months.

name: MOSAIC OF SUBCULTURES
problem: Modern cities' uniformity and lack of differentiation eliminates diversity in lifestyles and inhibits the development of individual character.
solution: Take all possible measures to enhance the cultural diversity of the city by fracturing the urban area into a complex mosaic of distinct subcultures, each with its own defined territory and empowered to create its own unique lifestyle. Ensure these subcultures remain small enough that individuals can easily access the variety of lifestyles present in neighboring subcultures.

name: SCATTERED WORK
problem: The forced separation between residential and workplace environments creates profound divisions in people's psychological well-being.
solution: Utilize zoning regulations, community planning, financial incentives, and all available mechanisms to distribute workplaces throughout the entire city. Restrict large concentrations of workplaces that lack surrounding residential areas. Similarly, prevent large residential developments that lack integrated workplaces.

name: MAGIC OF THE CITY
problem: Most people appreciate the unique energy and vitality of major urban centers. However, suburban sprawl makes this urban experience inaccessible to all but those fortunate or wealthy enough to live near major city centers.
solution: Make urban vibrancy accessible to all metropolitan residents through regional planning policies that strictly limit downtown growth so that no single downtown serves more than 300,000 people. With this population threshold, downtown areas will naturally develop between two and nine miles apart from each other.

name: LOCAL TRANSPORT AREAS
problem: Automobiles provide individuals with exceptional mobility and expanded opportunities. However, they also damage the environment so severely that they undermine community social interactions.
solution: Divide urban regions into local transportation zones, each measuring between 1 and 2 miles across, encircled by ring roads. Within these zones, develop networks of minor local roads and pathways for pedestrians, cyclists, equestrians, and local vehicles. Construct major roads that facilitate efficient car and truck access to and from the ring roads, but design them to make internal local travel deliberately slower and less convenient.

name: COMMUNITY OF 7000
problem: Citizens cannot meaningfully participate in governance when communities exceed 5,000-10,000 people.
solution: Restructure city governance to delegate local authority to communities of 5,000 to 10,000 residents. Where possible, use natural geographic features and historical boundaries to define these communities. Empower each community to initiate, decide, and implement matters directly affecting their area: land use decisions, housing policy, maintenance responsibilities, street management, parks administration, policing, education, welfare services, and neighborhood amenities.

name: SUBCULTURE BOUNDARY
problem: A diverse mosaic of subcultures requires hundreds of distinct cultural groups to thrive at full intensity while existing in close proximity. However, subcultures have specific environmental needs and can only fully flourish when protected from neighboring influences by physical separation.
solution: Create physical dividers between adjacent subcultures using a land buffer at least 200 feet wide. Allow these boundaries to be either natural elements - wilderness, agricultural land, bodies of water - or constructed features - railways, major roadways, parks, schools, residential zones. At the junction points between subcultures, develop shared spaces and communal facilities that provide connection points for both communities.

name: IDENTIFIABLE NEIGHBORHOOD
problem: Humans require clearly defined spatial territories to establish a sense of belonging.
solution: Assist communities in defining distinct neighborhoods with diameters not exceeding 300 yards and populations limited to 400 or 500 residents. In established cities, encourage residents to self-organize into such neighborhood units. Grant these neighborhoods partial autonomy regarding taxation and land use regulation. Design major roadways to bypass rather than bisect these neighborhoods.

name: NEIGHBORHOOD BOUNDARY
problem: A robust boundary is fundamental to neighborhood identity. When boundaries are insufficiently defined, neighborhoods struggle to maintain their distinctive character.
solution: Support the development of clear boundaries between adjacent neighborhoods to preserve their unique identities. Create these boundaries by reducing street connections and restricting neighborhood access - cutting the number of through streets by at least 50%. Establish gateway features at the points where access routes cross these boundaries, and make the boundary zone wide enough to accommodate shared facilities and meeting spaces used by multiple neighboring communities.

name: WEB OF PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION
section: Town and Country: Development of networks
problem: Public transportation networks—including airplanes, helicopters, hovercraft, trains, boats, ferries, buses, taxis, mini-trains, carts, ski-lifts, and moving sidewalks—can only function effectively when well-integrated. However, integration rarely occurs because the separate agencies managing different transportation modes have no motivation to coordinate with each other.
solution:
Prioritize interchanges over transportation lines. Develop incentives that encourage all transportation modes—from airplanes and helicopters to elevators and travelators—to design their routes around these interchanges. The goal is to gradually create hubs where multiple transportation types converge. Empower local communities to control these interchanges, allowing them to grant contracts exclusively to transportation providers willing to serve these connection points.
Give the local communities control over their interchanges so that they can implement the pattern by giving contracts only to those transportation companies which are willing to serve these interchanges.

name: RING ROADS
section: Town and Country: Development of networks
problem: Modern society requires high-speed roadways, but they must be designed and positioned carefully to prevent destruction of communities and natural landscapes.
solution:
Place high speed roads (freeways and other major arteries) so that:
1. At least one high speed road lies tangent to each local transport area.
2. Each local transport area has at least one side not bounded by a high speed road, but directly open to the countryside.
3. The road is always sunken, or shielded along its length by berms, or earth, or industrial buildings, to protect the nearby neighborhoods from noise.

name: NETWORK OF LEARNING
section: Town and Country: Development of networks
problem: Teaching-focused societies produce passive individuals who struggle to think or act independently. Creative, self-directed individuals can only develop in environments that prioritize learning over teaching.
solution: Instead of the lock-step of compulsory schooling in a fixed place, work in piecemeal ways to decentralize the process of learning and enrich it through contact with many places and people all over the city: workshops, teachers at home or walking through the city, professionals willing to take on the young as helpers, older children teaching younger children, museums, youth groups traveling, scholarly seminars, industrial workshops, old people, and so on. Conceive of all these situations as forming the backbone of the learning process; survey all these situations, describe them, and publish them as the city's "curriculum"; then let students, children, their families and neighborhoods weave together for themselves the situations that comprise their "school" paying as they go with standard vouchers, raised by community tax. Build new educational facilities in a way which extends and enriches this network.

name: WEB OF SHOPPING
section: Town and Country: Development of networks
problem: Retail establishments frequently position themselves in locations that neither adequately serve customer needs nor ensure their own business viability.
solution:
When you locate any individual shop, follow a three-step procedure:
1. Identify all other shops which offer the service you are interested in; locate them on the map.
2. Identify and map the location of potential consumers. Wherever possible, indicate the density or total number of potential consumers in any given area.
3. Look for the biggest gap in the existing web of shops in those areas where there are potential consumers.
4. Within the gap in the web of similar shops, locate your shop next to the largest cluster of other kinds of shops.

name: MINI-BUSES
section: Town and Country: Development of networks
problem: Effective public transportation needs to connect any point within a metropolitan area to any other point.
solution: Establish a system of small taxi-like buses, carrying up to six people each, radio-controlled, on call by telephone, able to provide point-to-point service according to the passengers' needs, and supplemented by a computer system which guarantees minimum detours, and minimum waiting times. Make bus stops for the mini-buses every 600 feet in each direction, and equip these bus stops with a phone for dialing a bus.

name: FOUR-STORY LIMIT
problem: Substantial research demonstrates that tall buildings have negative psychological impacts on their inhabitants.
solution: In any urban area, no matter how dense, keep the majority of buildings four stories high or less. It is possible that certain buildings should exceed this limit, but they should never be buildings for human habitation.

name: NINE PER CENT PARKING
problem: When too much land is dedicated to parking facilities, it degrades the overall environment.
solution: Do not allow more than 9 per cent of the land in any given area to be used for parking. In order to prevent the "bunching" of parking in huge neglected areas, it is necessary for a town or a community to subdivide its land into "parking zones" no larger than 10 acres each and to apply the same rule in each zone.

name: PARALLEL ROADS
problem: Traditional grid-pattern street layouts have become obsolete as congestion chokes urban areas. Despite freeway speeds of 60 mph, average cross-town trips move at only 10-15 mph.
solution: Within a local transport area build no intersecting major roads at all; instead, build a system of parallel and alternating one-way roads to carry traffic to the ring roads. In existing towns, create this structure piecemeal, by gradually making major streets one-way and closing cross streets. Keep parallel roads at least 100 yards apart (to make room for neighborhoods between them) and no more than 300 or 400 yards apart.

name: SACRED SITES
problem: People lose their spiritual connections and historical roots when their physical surroundings fail to support these essential connections.
solution: Whether the sacred sites are large or small, whether they are at the center of the towns, in neighborhoods, or in the deepest countryside, establish ordinances which will protect them absolutely - so that our roots in the visible surroundings cannot be violated.

name: ACCESS TO WATER
problem: Humans have an innate desire to be near significant water bodies. However, the very movement of people toward water can ultimately degrade or destroy these natural resources.
solution: When natural bodies of water occur near human settlements, treat them with great respect. Always preserve a belt of common land, immediately beside the water. And allow dense settlements to come right down to the water only at infrequent intervals along the water's edge.

name: LIFE CYCLE
problem:
All the world's a stage,
And all the men and women merely players:
They have their exits and their entrances;
And one man in his time plays many parts,
His acts being seven ages.

name: MEN AND WOMEN
problem: In 1970s towns, environments are divided along gender lines. Suburbs are feminine domains while workplaces are masculine; kindergartens are women's spaces while professional schools belong to men; supermarkets are for women, hardware stores for men.
solution: Make certain that each piece of the environment - each building, open space, neighborhood, and work community - is made with a blend of both men's and women's instincts. Keep this balance of masculine and feminine in mind for every project at every scale, from the kitchen to the steel mill.

name: ECCENTRIC NUCLEUS
problem: The haphazard distribution of local densities creates confusion about community identity and leads to disorganized land use patterns.
solution:
Support development and density concentration to create a distinct pattern of high and low-density areas following these guidelines:
1. Consider the town as a collection of communities of 7000. These communities will be between '/4 mile across and 2 miles across, according to their overall density.
2. Mark that point in the boundary of each community which is closest to the nearest major urban center. This point will be the peak of the density, and the core of the "eccentric" nucleus.
3. Allow the high density to bulge in from the boundary, toward the center of gravity of the community, thus enlarging the eccentric nucleus toward the center.
4. Continue this high density to form a ridge around the boundary in horseshoe fashion - with the length of the horseshoe dependent on the overall mean gross density, at that part of the city, and the bulge of the horseshoe toward the center of the region, so that the horseshoes form a gradient, according to their position in the region. Those close to a major downtown are almost complete; those further away are only half complete; and those furthest from centers are shrunken to a point.

name: DENSITY RINGS
problem: Residents desire proximity to services for excitement and convenience, yet simultaneously seek distance from these services for tranquility and green space. The varying balance of these contradictory needs among individuals collectively shapes the density gradient in neighborhood housing.
solution: After establishing a clear community nucleus, establish concentric rings of decreasing housing density around this center. If necessary, select densities from the provided table. However, when possible, it is far better to implement the density rings game, deriving these densities from the preferences of the actual future residents of the community.

name: ACTIVITY NODES
problem: When community facilities are dispersed individually throughout the city, they contribute little to urban vitality.
solution: Develop activity nodes throughout the community, spaced approximately 300 yards apart. Begin by identifying existing locations where activity naturally concentrates. Then adjust the community path layout to route as many paths as possible through these locations, enabling each spot to function as a "node" within the path network. At each node's center, create a small public square surrounded by complementary community facilities and shops that support each other.

name: PROMENADE
problem: Every subculture requires a central space for its public interactions—a location where people can observe others and be observed themselves.
solution: Support the progressive development of a promenade at each community's core, connecting the main activity nodes and positioned centrally so all community points are within a 10-minute walk. Place primary attractions at both ends to maintain continuous movement along the promenade.

name: SHOPPING STREET
problem: Shopping centers require access near major traffic arteries, yet shoppers themselves don't benefit from traffic—they need tranquility, comfort, convenience, and pedestrian access from the surrounding area.
solution: Foster the development of local shopping centers as short pedestrian streets, oriented perpendicular to major roads and accessible from these roads. Position parking behind the shops to allow vehicles direct access from the main road without disrupting the shopping street experience.

name: NIGHT LIFE
problem: Most urban activities cease at night; those that remain open contribute little to the city's nightlife unless they're clustered together.
solution: Integrate shops, entertainment venues, and services that operate at night, together with hotels, bars, and all-night diners to create vibrant nightlife centers. These areas should be well-illuminated, secure, and animated, concentrating evening pedestrian activity in specific locations throughout the city. Encourage these nightlife hubs to be evenly distributed across the urban area.

name: INTERCHANGE
problem: Transit interchanges serve a vital function in public transportation networks. A public transportation system cannot remain viable unless its interchanges function effectively.
solution:
At every interchange in the web of transportation follow these principles:
1. Surround the interchange with workplaces and housing types which specially need public transportation.
2. Keep the interior of the interchange continuous with the exterior pedestrian network, and maintain this continuity by building in small shops and kiosks and by keeping parking to one side.
3. Keep the transfer distance between different modes of transport down to 300 feet wherever possible, with an absolute maximum of 600 feet.

name: HOUSEHOLD MIX
problem: No single life cycle stage is self-sufficient or capable of meeting all social needs.
solution: Promote development toward diverse household compositions in each neighborhood and cluster, enabling one-person households, couples, families with children, and communal living arrangements to exist side by side.

name: DEGREES OF PUBLICNESS
problem: Individual preferences vary, and how people choose to position their homes within a neighborhood represents one of the most fundamental differences.
solution: Establish a clear distinction between three home types: those on secluded backwaters, those on busy thoroughfares, and intermediate options. Ensure that homes on quiet backwaters are situated along winding paths and are physically sheltered; homes in more public locations should be on busy streets with frequent foot traffic and relatively exposed to passersby. Position the intermediate homes on paths halfway between these extremes. Aim for approximately equal numbers of each home type in every neighborhood.

name: HOUSE CLUSTER
problem: Residents won't feel at ease in their homes unless groups of houses form recognizable clusters with communally owned public spaces between them.
solution: Configure houses to create approximate but discernible clusters of 8 to 12 households surrounding shared land and pathways. Design these clusters to be permeable, allowing people to walk through without feeling like intruders.

name: ROW HOUSES
problem: At densities of 15 to 30 houses per acre, row houses become necessary. However, conventional row houses often suffer from dark interiors and monotonous, identical designs.
solution: For row house developments, position homes along pedestrian paths running perpendicular to local roads and parking areas, providing each house with an extended frontage and minimal depth.

name: HOUSING HILL
problem: In highly desirable central locations within towns, housing density often reaches 30-50 households per acre. Unfortunately, apartment buildings that achieve this density typically lack personality and human connection.
solution: When building housing that exceeds 30 dwellings per net acre, or reaches three to four stories in height, construct a terraced hill formation. Design these structures with stepped terraces that face southward, and incorporate a prominent central stairway also oriented south, leading to a shared garden area...

name: OLD PEOPLE EVERYWHERE
problem: Elderly people benefit from the company of other seniors, but they also require interaction with younger generations, just as younger people need exposure to the elderly.
solution:
Establish housing for approximately 50 elderly residents in each neighborhood. Arrange these dwellings in three concentric zones...
1. A central core with cooking and nursing provided.
2. Cottages near the core.
3. Cottages further out from the core, mixed among the other houses of the neighborhood, but never more than 200 yards from the core.
... in such a way that the 50 houses together form a single coherent swarm, with its own clear center, but interlocked at its periphery with other ordinary houses of the neighborhood.

name: WORK COMMUNITY
problem: With people spending equal amounts of time at work and at home—eight hours each—there's no reason why workplaces shouldn't foster the same sense of community that homes do.
solution: Create work communities consisting of clusters of smaller workplaces arranged around their own courtyards, which in turn surround a larger common square or courtyard containing shops and dining options. Limit these work communities to no more than 10-20 individual workplaces total.

name: INDUSTRIAL RIBBON
problem: Current zoning laws create an artificial separation between industry and other aspects of urban life, contributing to the sterile, disconnected feeling of many residential neighborhoods.
solution: Arrange industrial areas in ribbon formations between 200 and 500 feet wide that serve as boundaries between communities. Divide these ribbons into varied blocks ranging from 1 to 25 acres in size. Design the edges of these industrial ribbons as interface zones where community residents can benefit from and engage with industrial activities.

name: UNIVERSITY AS A MARKETPLACE
problem: Learning opportunities are stifled by universities that are isolated, exclusive and governed by rigid policies about who can teach courses and how education must be structured.
solution: Reimagine the university as an educational marketplace open to people of all ages who can attend full-time, part-time, or on a course-by-course basis. Create a system where anyone can offer a class and anyone can take a class. Physically structure the university with a central crossroads containing main buildings and offices, with meeting rooms and laboratories extending outward along pedestrian streets, gradually becoming more integrated with the surrounding town.

name: LOCAL TOWN HALL
problem: Effective local governance and community control can only exist when communities have dedicated physical spaces that serve as centers for political activity.
solution: Develop a small town hall for each community of 7,000 residents, and even for individual neighborhoods, situated near the community's busiest intersection. Design the building with three distinct sections: a space for public discussions, an area for public services surrounding this forum, and rentable space for temporary community initiatives.

name: NECKLACE OF COMMUNITY PROJECTS
problem: A local town hall cannot truly represent and connect with its surrounding community unless it is integrated with and surrounded by grassroots community activities and initiatives created by the residents themselves.
solution: Facilitate the development of shop-sized spaces surrounding the local town hall and other community buildings. These spaces should face busy walkways and be rented at minimal cost to various community groups for political activities, experimental services, research initiatives, and advocacy work, without imposing ideological restrictions.

name: MARKET OF MANY SHOPS
problem: While people naturally desire the convenience of purchasing all their food and household goods under one roof, markets under single management like supermarkets often offer bland products and provide little joy in the shopping experience.
solution: Create regular marketplaces composed of numerous independent and specialized shops (such as cheese, meat, grain, and fruit vendors) rather than modern supermarkets. Construct only the basic market infrastructure—a roof, columns defining walkways, and essential utilities. Within this framework, allow individual shops to design their own environments according to their specific needs and preferences.

name: HEALTH CENTER
problem: By basic biological standards, over 90 percent of people in typical neighborhoods suffer from some form of poor health, which traditional hospitals and medical approaches cannot effectively address.
solution: Incrementally establish a network of small health centers throughout the city, approximately one per 7,000-person community. Equip these centers to treat common physical and mental health issues for both children and adults, but organize them primarily around recreational and educational activities that promote good health, such as swimming and dancing.

name: HOUSING IN BETWEEN
problem: When residential and non-residential areas are strictly divided, the non-residential sections tend to deteriorate into slums.
solution: Integrate housing into the fabric of commercial, educational, industrial, and public service areas—all those zones that attract daytime activity but are traditionally considered "non-residential." These homes may be arranged in rows, form "hills" with shops below, or stand independently, as long as they blend with other functions to create truly "lived-in" neighborhoods throughout.

name: LOOPED LOCAL ROADS
problem: Residents are universally opposed to fast-moving through traffic passing by their homes.
solution: Design local road systems in loop configurations that prevent cars without destinations on the loop from using it as a shortcut. Limit each loop to serving no more than 50 vehicles and keep road widths minimal—17 to 20 feet is sufficient.

name: T JUNCTIONS
problem: Intersections where two roads cross directly have significantly higher accident rates than T-shaped junctions.
solution: Configure road systems so that when two roads meet at the same grade, they form three-way T junctions as close to 90 degrees as possible. Minimize four-way intersections and avoid crossing traffic patterns.

name: GREEN STREETS
problem: The world contains an excess of hot, hard asphalt surfaces. Local access roads only need minimal hard surfaces for vehicle wheels—the majority can remain natural and green.
solution: On local roads closed to through traffic, cover the entire surface with grass and embed occasional paving stones to accommodate vehicle wheels. Remove the distinction between street and sidewalk. Where homes connect to the street, add more paving stones or gravel to allow cars to turn into private properties.

lower_patterns.name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES

name: NETWORK OF PATHS AND CARS
problem: Automobiles pose significant hazards to people walking, yet ironically, the intersection points between pedestrian and vehicular traffic are precisely where human activities tend to concentrate.
solution: Unless traffic volumes are at the extremes (either very dense or minimal), design pedestrian walkways perpendicular to roadways rather than parallel to them. This gradually creates a second, distinct network that intersects with but remains separate from the road system. Implementation can be incremental—installing one path at a time—but always position them through the center of blocks so they traverse across roads rather than alongside them.

name: MAIN GATEWAYS
problem: When residents identify a particular section of town as a distinct precinct—regardless of its size—this recognition is strengthened and the area's character is enhanced if the entry points along its boundaries are clearly delineated.
solution: Emphasize every meaningful boundary within the urban environment—whether it defines a building cluster, neighborhood, or precinct—by establishing prominent gateway structures where primary access paths intersect with these borders.

name: ROAD CROSSING
problem: At intersections where footpaths meet roadways, vehicles exert a psychological dominance over pedestrians, intimidating them even when pedestrians legally have the right-of-way.
solution: Whenever a pedestrian route crosses a road with sufficient traffic to cause more than a two-second delay for crossing, create a "knuckle" at the intersection: reduce the road width to only what's needed for through traffic; elevate the pedestrian crossing approximately one foot above the road surface; install refuge islands between traffic lanes; incorporate a gentle slope approaching the crossing (maximum 1:6 ratio); and mark the pathway with a canopy or shelter to enhance visibility.

name: RAISED WALK
problem: In urban areas where rapid-moving vehicles and pedestrians intersect, the automobiles overwhelm those on foot. Cars assert dominance, making pedestrians feel diminished and vulnerable.
solution: Our analysis indicates that pedestrian pathways alongside roads with fast-moving traffic should be elevated approximately 18 inches above the roadway. Install a low wall, railing, or balustrade along the edge to define this boundary. Implement this raised walkway on just one side of the road, making it as spacious as possible.

name: BIKE PATHS AND RACKS
problem: Bicycles offer affordable, healthy, and environmentally friendly transportation, yet our built environment isn't designed to accommodate them. On streets, cyclists face danger from motor vehicles; on footpaths, pedestrians face hazards from cyclists.
solution: Develop a dedicated network of bike paths with distinctive, easily identifiable surfacing (such as red asphalt). Where feasible, route these paths alongside local roads or major pedestrian walkways. When a bike path runs parallel to a local road, keep it at road level—preferably on the sunnier side; when it runs alongside a pedestrian path, maintain separation and position it slightly lower (a few inches below the pedestrian level). Extend this bike path network to within 100 feet of all buildings and install bicycle racks near each building's main entrance.

name: CHILDREN IN THE CITY
problem: Children require opportunities to explore the broader adult world surrounding them to develop into adults themselves. However, contemporary urban environments present such dangers that children are often prevented from freely exploring their surroundings.
solution: As a component of the bicycle path network, establish a particularly secure route system completely separated from automobile traffic, with illumination and bridges at crossings, lined with residences and businesses to ensure constant surveillance. Design this pathway to traverse through every neighborhood, allowing children access without crossing major roadways. Extend these paths throughout the city, guiding children through pedestrian streets and into various workplaces—workshops, factories, warehouses, transportation hubs, printing facilities, bakeries—exposing them to the fascinating "hidden" aspects of urban life and enabling them to roam securely on their bicycles and tricycles.

name: CARNIVAL
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Just as individuals experience fantastic dreams to release inner forces that ordinary experiences cannot contain, cities similarly require outlets for extraordinary expression.
solution: Designate a specific area of the town as a carnival zone featuring eccentric sideshows, competitions, performances, displays, tournaments, dancing, music, street theater, clowns, cross-dressing, and unusual events that allow people to express their eccentricities. Create a wide pedestrian thoroughfare through this district, flanked by booths and narrow alleyways. Establish an outdoor theater at one end, potentially connecting the stage directly to the carnival street so the two spaces can intermingle and enhance each other.

name: QUIET BACKS
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Those who work amid noise and constant human interaction in office environments need opportunities to refresh themselves in quieter, more natural settings.
solution: Create tranquil "back" areas for buildings in busy urban districts, removed from noise. Construct a walking path along this peaceful space, positioned far enough from buildings to receive abundant sunlight while remaining protected from noise by walls, distance, and structures. Ensure this path doesn't become a natural shortcut for heavy foot traffic, and connect it with other similar walkways to form an extended network of quiet passages that lead to local water features and green spaces.

name: ACCESSIBLE GREEN
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Humans require access to open green spaces and will utilize them regularly when they're nearby. However, if these areas are situated more than three minutes away, the distance becomes a psychological barrier that outweighs the perceived benefit.
solution: Establish public green spaces within a three-minute walking distance—approximately 750 feet—from every residence and workplace. This necessitates distributing green areas uniformly throughout the city at 1500-foot intervals. Ensure each green space measures at least 150 feet across and encompasses a minimum area of 60,000 square feet.

name: SMALL PUBLIC SQUARES
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Communities require public squares that function as their largest, most communal spaces. However, when these squares are oversized, they appear and feel abandoned.
solution: Design public squares significantly smaller than intuition might suggest—typically between 45 and 60 feet across, never exceeding 70 feet in width. This dimension applies specifically to the shorter axis; the square may certainly extend longer in its primary direction.

name: HIGH PLACES
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: The desire to ascend to elevated positions from which one can observe and survey the surrounding environment appears to be a fundamental human inclination.
solution: Incorporate periodic high vantage points as landmarks throughout the urban landscape. These may utilize natural topographical features, tower structures, or rooftop areas of the tallest local buildings—but in all cases, they should involve a physical climbing experience.

name: DANCING IN THE STREET
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: What factors have led to the disappearance of spontaneous street dancing in contemporary society?
solution: In promenades, squares, and evening gathering places, construct a slightly elevated platform to serve as a bandstand for street musicians and local performers. Provide covering for this structure and consider incorporating small refreshment stalls at ground level. Surround the bandstand with paved areas intended for dancing, with no entrance fees required.

name: POOLS AND STREAMS
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Humanity evolved from aquatic origins; our bodies consist primarily of water; and water plays an essential psychological role in our lives. We require consistent access to water throughout our environment, which is impossible without respecting water in all its forms. Unfortunately, urban settings have largely eliminated accessible water features.
solution: Conserve natural water bodies and watercourses, allowing them to flow through urban areas; create pathways alongside them and footbridges for crossing. Utilize streams as natural dividing elements within the city, with vehicular crossings limited to occasional bridges. Where possible, collect rainwater in open channels and direct it along pedestrian routes and residential frontages. In locations lacking natural flowing water, install public fountains in streets.

name: BIRTH PLACES
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: Any approach that treats childbirth as an illness seems fundamentally incompatible with fostering a healthy society.
solution: Establish community birth centers where women go to deliver their children—facilities specifically designed to honor childbirth as a natural, significant life event. These centers should welcome entire families for prenatal education and care, and create environments where fathers and midwives can actively participate throughout labor and delivery.

name: HOLY GROUND
section: Town and Country: Public open spaces
problem: What defines a church or temple at its core? Beyond being a place for worship, spirituality, and reflection, it fundamentally serves as a threshold from a human perspective. People enter the world through it, depart life through it, and cross through it at each significant transition in their lives.
solution: Within each neighborhood and community, designate a sacred location as consecrated ground. Create a series of nested precincts, each one marked by a gateway, becoming progressively more private and sacred as one moves inward. The innermost sanctum should only be accessible by passing through all outer precincts.

name: COMMON LAND
problem: A social system cannot endure without shared common land.
solution: Allocate 25 percent of land in housing clusters to common areas that directly touch or are very near the homes that share this space. Important: be cautious about automobiles; don't allow them to dominate this common land.

name: CONNECTED PLAY
problem: Children who don't engage in sufficient play with other children during their first five years face a significant risk of developing mental health issues later in life.
solution: Design common land, paths, gardens, and bridges to connect at least 64 households through a continuous stretch of land that doesn't cross traffic. Designate this connected area as the play space for children from these households.

name: PUBLIC OUTDOOR ROOM
problem: Modern towns and neighborhoods lack comfortable public spaces along streets where people can linger comfortably for extended periods.
solution: Convert a section of common land in every neighborhood and work community into an outdoor room - a partially enclosed space with some roof and columns but without walls, perhaps with a trellis. Position it alongside an important path and within view of numerous homes and workshops.

name: GRAVE SITES
problem: People who ignore death cannot truly be alive. In a society that encourages its members to live fully, the presence of the dead among the living must be acknowledged as a daily reality.
solution: Rather than creating large centralized cemeteries, distribute grave sites throughout the community - in park corners, along paths, in gardens, near gateways - where memorials for the deceased can be placed with inscriptions and mementos celebrating their lives. Provide each grave site with an edge, a path, and a quiet corner for sitting. By tradition, this land is considered sacred.

name: STILL WATER
problem: To truly connect with water, swimming is essential; for daily swimming to be possible, pools and ponds must be widely distributed throughout the city so everyone can reach one within minutes.
solution: Provide a body of still water - pond or pool - for swimming in every neighborhood. Keep it publicly accessible at all times, but design the entrance to be only from the shallow side, with depth increasing gradually from just one or two inches.

name: LOCAL SPORTS
problem: The human body doesn't deteriorate from use - rather, it declines when not used.
solution: Distribute facilities for team and individual sports throughout every neighborhood and work community: tennis, squash, table tennis, swimming, billiards, basketball, dancing, gymnasium, etc. Make these activities visible to passersby as an invitation to participate.

name: ADVENTURE PLAYGROUND
problem: A homemade castle constructed by children using cardboard, rocks, and branches has far more value than a thousand perfectly manufactured castles made for them in a factory.
solution: Establish a playground in each neighborhood that's not a finished space with asphalt and swings, but rather a place filled with diverse raw materials - nets, boxes, barrels, trees, ropes, simple tools, frames, grass, and water - where children can create and recreate their own play environments.

name: ANIMALS
problem: Animals are as essential to nature as trees, grass, and flowers. Evidence also suggests that interaction with animals may play a crucial role in children's emotional development.
solution: Create legal provisions allowing people to keep animals on private lots or in private stables. Establish a protected, fenced common area with grass, trees, and water where animals can freely graze. Design at least one movement system in the neighborhood that is completely free of asphalt, where animal droppings can fall naturally without requiring cleanup.

name: THE FAMILY
problem: The nuclear family by itself is not a sustainable social structure.
solution:
Implement processes encouraging groups of 8 to 12 people to form communal households. Key morphological elements include:
1. Private spaces for individuals and subgroups within the extended family: couple's areas, private rooms, and smaller family sub-households.
2. Shared space for communal functions: cooking, working, gardening, and childcare.
3. A central gathering place at the important crossroads of the site where the entire group can meet and sit together.

name: HOUSE FOR A SMALL FAMILY
problem: In a small family home, the most crucial aspect is the relationship between adults and children.
solution: Design the house with three distinct areas: a realm for parents, a realm for children, and a common area. Create these three spaces to be roughly similar in size, with the common area being slightly larger than the others.

name: HOUSE FOR A COUPLE
problem: In a small household shared by two people, the primary challenge is ensuring each person has sufficient opportunity for solitude and privacy.
solution: Design a couple's house with two distinct types of spaces - a shared couple's realm and individual private worlds. Envision the shared area as being both semi-public and semi-intimate, while the private spaces should be completely individual and personal.

name: HOUSE FOR ONE PERSON
problem: For a single-person household that's part of a larger community, the primary challenge becomes the necessity for straightforward, uncomplicated living.
solution: Design a single-person dwelling as a space of complete simplicity: essentially a one-room structure with various sized alcoves surrounding it. At its most efficient form, the entire dwelling may only measure between 300 to 400 square feet.

name: YOUR OWN HOME
problem: Individuals cannot truly achieve comfort and health in housing they do not own. All rental arrangements – whether from private owners or public agencies – undermine the natural processes that enable stable, self-healing community formation.
solution: Take all possible measures to render traditional rental arrangements impossible and even unlawful. Provide each household with its own residence and sufficient space for gardening. In defining ownership, emphasize control rather than financial possession. When possible, favor ownership structures that give residents control over their dwellings while preventing financial speculation. In all instances, grant people both the legal authority and practical opportunity to modify and repair their own spaces. Apply this principle with special attention to high-density apartment buildings: construct apartments so each unit has a garden or vegetable-growing terrace, allowing families to build, modify, and expand their homes according to their needs.

name: SELF-GOVERNING WORKSHOPS AND OFFICES
problem: Work becomes unfulfilling when an individual feels like merely a replaceable part in a larger machine.
solution: Support the development of self-governing workspaces containing 5 to 20 workers. Make each group independent regarding organization, style, relationships with other groups, personnel decisions, and work scheduling. For complex work requiring larger organizations, these smaller work groups can collaborate and form federations to create sophisticated products and services.

name: SMALL SERVICES WITHOUT RED TAPE
problem: Public services and departments become ineffective when they grow too large. In oversized organizations, human elements disappear, bureaucracy takes over, and red tape dominates operations.
solution:
Within any public service institution:
1. Maximize each service or department's autonomy.
2. Limit staff size to no more than 12 members per service.
3. House each service in a clearly identifiable portion of the building.
4. Ensure each service has direct access to a public thoroughfare.

name: OFFICE CONNECTIONS
problem: When office sections are excessively distant from each other, necessary movement between them diminishes; when separated by more than one floor, interdepartmental communication virtually ceases.
solution: When determining interdepartmental distances, calculate daily trips between each department pair; identify the "nuisance distance" from the relevant graph; then ensure the physical separation between departments remains below this nuisance threshold. Consider one flight of stairs equivalent to approximately 100 feet, and two flights equivalent to roughly 300 feet.

name: MASTER AND APPRENTICES
problem: The most effective learning occurs when an individual learns while assisting someone with genuine expertise.
solution: Structure work in every workplace, industry, and office to integrate work and learning seamlessly. View each task as a learning opportunity. To accomplish this, organize work around a master-apprentice tradition, supporting this social framework with workspace divisions into spatial clusters – one for each master and their apprentices – providing environments for collaborative work and interaction.

name: TEENAGE SOCIETY
problem: Adolescence represents the transition period between childhood and adulthood. Traditional societies mark this transition with rituals that address the psychological requirements of this change. However, modern "high schools" completely fail to facilitate this important passage.
solution: Supplant the conventional "high school" with an institution that genuinely models adult society, where students assume primary responsibility for their learning and social interactions, with clearly defined roles and disciplinary structures. Provide adult guidance for both educational content and social organization, but keep these elements largely under student control whenever feasible.

name: SHOPFRONT SCHOOLS
problem: Children around ages 6 or 7 develop a strong need for experiential learning and making meaningful contributions to a community beyond their home. In the right environment, these developmental needs naturally lead to acquiring fundamental skills and learning habits.
solution: Rather than constructing large public schools for children aged 7 to 12, establish small independent schools one at a time. Keep each school small to maintain low overhead costs and achieve a 1:10 teacher-student ratio. Position the school in the community's public area, with a shopfront design and three or four rooms.

name: CHILDREN'S HOME
problem: Caring for young children represents a far more profound and essential social concern than terms like "baby-sitting" or "child care" suggest.
solution: Establish a children's home – a secondary home for children – in each neighborhood: a spacious, accommodating house or workplace where children can stay for brief periods or extended stays. At least one caretaker must reside on the premises; it should operate continuously, be accessible to children of all ages, and be clearly run as a second family for the children – not merely a child-minding facility.

name: INDIVIDUALLY OWNED SHOPS
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Shops become impersonal, generic, and disconnected when they are too large or managed by absent owners.
solution: Take actions to promote individually owned shop development. Issue business licenses only to businesses owned by the people who actively operate and manage the store. Approve new commercial construction permits only for buildings that incorporate numerous very small rental spaces.

name: STREET CAFE
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Street cafes offer a distinctive urban environment where people can relax legitimately, observe others, and be seen while watching daily life unfold around them.
solution: Foster the development of neighborhood cafes throughout the city. Design them as intimate spaces with multiple rooms that open onto busy walkways, creating places where patrons can enjoy a coffee or drink while observing street activity. Extend the cafe's tables from the interior directly onto the street, blending indoor and outdoor spaces.

name: CORNER GROCERY
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: There's a common misconception that people no longer wish to walk to neighborhood stores. This assumption is incorrect.
solution: Ensure every neighborhood has at least one corner grocery store near its center. Distribute these stores every 200 to 800 yards based on population density, with each serving approximately 1,000 residents. Position them at corners where pedestrian traffic is high. Integrate the stores with housing so proprietors can live above or adjacent to their businesses.

name: BEER HALL
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Where can individuals gather to sing, drink, shout, and release their troubles in a communal setting?
solution: Create at least one large gathering space within the community that can accommodate several hundred people. This venue should serve beer and wine, feature music, and offer multiple activities that encourage people to continuously move between different areas, fostering interaction and socialization.

name: TRAVELER'S INN
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Travelers staying in unfamiliar places remain part of the human community and need social connection. There's no reason they should be isolated in solitary rooms watching television, as typically happens in roadside motels.
solution: Design traveler's inns that provide overnight accommodation while drawing vitality from the community of guests present each evening. Keep the scale intimate—about 30 to 40 guests per inn. Offer communal meals and include a spacious area surrounded by beds in alcoves, encouraging interaction rather than isolation.

name: BUS STOP
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Bus stops need to be easily identifiable and pleasant, with sufficient activity nearby to create a sense of comfort and security for waiting passengers.
solution: Design bus stops as small centers of public life. Integrate them into neighborhood gateways, work areas, and town districts. Position them to function alongside other amenities—at minimum, include a newsstand, maps, outdoor shelter, and seating. Consider combining with corner groceries, smoke shops, coffee bars, tree-shaded areas, designated road crossings, public restrooms, or small squares to create vibrant micro-environments.

name: FOOD STANDS
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: Many social habits and institutions are supported by the availability of simple, affordable food from street vendors that people can access while traveling between shopping, work, and social activities.
solution: Focus food stands at intersections of pedestrian paths and vehicular routes. These can be mobile carts, small kiosks, or integrated into building facades with semi-open connections to the street, making them easily accessible to passersby.

name: SLEEPING IN PUBLIC
section: Town and Country: Gathering places and shops
problem: A public space—whether park, lobby, or porch—demonstrates its success when visitors feel comfortable enough to fall asleep there.
solution: Provide abundant benches, comfortable nooks, and corners where people can sit on the ground or recline comfortably in sand. Create these spaces with relative shelter from main circulation paths, perhaps elevated slightly, with seating and grassy areas where people can relax with a newspaper and naturally drift into a nap.

name: BUILDING COMPLEX
problem: A building cannot truly serve human needs unless it comprises smaller buildings or components that reflect its internal social organization.
solution:
Avoid constructing large monolithic structures. When possible, translate your building program into a complex of elements that express the actual social relationships of the situation. In lower-density areas, this may manifest as clusters of small buildings linked by arcades, paths, bridges, shared gardens, and walls.
In higher-density settings, a single building can function as a complex if its key components are clearly defined while remaining part of a unified three-dimensional composition.
Even smaller structures like houses can be conceptualized as "building complexes"—perhaps featuring elevated sections with wings and adjoining cottages.

name: NUMBER OF STORIES
problem: Within the established four-story height limit, what is the optimal height for your specific buildings?
solution: Begin by calculating your required square footage of built space, then divide by the site area to determine the floor area ratio. Select your building height based on this ratio and the heights of surrounding structures, using the provided reference table. Under no circumstances should you build on more than 50 percent of the available land.

name: SHIELDED PARKING
problem: Large parking structures filled with cars create inhospitable, lifeless environments that pedestrians avoid. Yet for drivers, parking entrances effectively serve as main entrances to buildings and need to remain visible.
solution:
Conceal substantial parking lots or garages behind natural barriers to shield vehicles and parking structures from external view. These screening elements might include buildings, connected houses, housing hills, earth berms, or shops.
Design the parking entrance as a natural gateway to the buildings it serves, positioned so that the main building entrance is clearly visible from the parking entrance point.

name: CIRCULATION REALMS
problem: Disorientation is a significant issue in many contemporary building complexes. People frequently lose their bearings, causing considerable mental distress.
solution: Configure large buildings and building clusters so that reaching any interior destination requires passing through a sequence of distinct realms. Each realm should be marked by a gateway, with spaces becoming progressively smaller as one moves deeper into the sequence. Select realms that can be easily named, enabling clear directional instructions by simply identifying which realms to traverse.

name: MAIN BUILDING
problem: A building complex without a focal point resembles a body without a head—it lacks essential organization and identity.
solution:
For any building group, identify which structure houses the most essential function—the building that represents the soul of the group as a human institution. Develop this as the main building, giving it a central position and higher roof to establish its prominence.
Even in dense complexes that form a single building, make the primary section taller and more visually prominent than surrounding elements, ensuring that attention naturally gravitates toward the most important part.

name: PEDESTRIAN STREET
problem: The casual social interactions that occur when people naturally encounter one another in public spaces represent one of the most fundamental forms of social cohesion in society.
solution: Configure buildings to create pedestrian streets with numerous entrances and open staircases leading directly from upper floors to the street level. Design the environment so that movement between rooms occurs outdoors when possible, not just movement between buildings, maximizing opportunities for spontaneous encounters.

name: BUILDING THOROUGHFARE
problem: Public building complexes that cannot be fully serviced by outdoor pedestrian walkways require an alternative to conventional corridors for indoor circulation.
solution: In situations where climate or density necessitates moving primary circulation routes indoors, construct them as building thoroughfares. Position each thoroughfare to function as a convenient shortcut, maintaining continuity with the external public streets and featuring wide, accessible entrances. Along the edges, incorporate windows, seating areas, counters, and entrances that extend into the hall, making the building's primary functions visible to passersby. Design these thoroughfares wider than standard corridors—minimum 11 feet, but preferably 15-20 feet wide—with high ceilings (at least 15 feet), potentially including glazed roofing and lower-height areas along the perimeter. In multi-story thoroughfares, walkways on different levels can be utilized to create these lower-height spaces along the edges.

name: FAMILY OF ENTRANCES
problem: Visitors to complexes containing multiple offices, services, workshops, or related housing units may become disoriented unless they can easily identify the entrance to their specific destination within the overall layout.
solution:
Organize entrances as a cohesive family. This approach requires:
1. Grouping entrances so they're collectively visible, with each entrance visible from all others.
2. Ensuring stylistic consistency across all entrances—for example, all designed as porches, all as gates within a wall, or all marked with similar doorway treatments.

name: SMALL PARKING LOTS
problem: Large-scale parking facilities significantly degrade the human experience of the landscape.
solution: Develop compact parking areas accommodating only 5-7 vehicles each, enclosed by garden walls, hedges, fences, embankments, and trees to render the vehicles nearly invisible from outside. Distribute these small lots with a minimum of 100 feet between them.

name: SITE REPAIR
problem: Construction should prioritize improving compromised areas rather than utilizing the most pristine portions of a site.
solution: Never place structures on the most aesthetically pleasing sections of land. Instead, take the opposite approach—view the site and its buildings as an integrated living ecosystem. Preserve the most valuable, beautiful, comfortable, and healthy areas in their natural state, and concentrate new construction on the least appealing sections of the site.

name: SOUTH FACING OUTDOORS
problem: In all but desert environments, people gravitate toward sunlit outdoor spaces and avoid shaded areas.
solution: Position buildings on the northern side of their associated outdoor spaces, keeping the outdoor areas to the south. Avoid creating deep bands of shade between buildings and the sunny portions of exterior spaces.

name: POSITIVE OUTDOOR SPACE
problem: Outdoor areas that are merely leftover spaces between buildings typically remain unused.
solution: Design all outdoor spaces surrounding and between buildings to have positive, intentional qualities. Provide each space with some degree of enclosure by using building wings, trees, hedges, fences, arcades, and trellised walkways to define its boundaries. This creates distinct entities with clear spatial definition rather than amorphous areas that dissipate around corners.

name: WINGS OF LIGHT
problem: Contemporary architecture often ignores natural illumination, relying predominantly on artificial lighting. However, buildings that minimize natural light as the primary illumination source create environments unsuitable for extended human occupation.
solution: Structure each building to divide into wings that roughly correspond to the most significant natural social groupings within the building. Design each wing to be elongated and as narrow as feasible—never exceeding 25 feet in width.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: CONNECTED BUILDINGS
problem: Buildings that stand in isolation reflect the fragmentation of an unhealthy society.
solution: Whenever possible, integrate your building with existing structures in the surrounding area. Avoid maintaining setbacks between buildings; instead, strive to design new structures as extensions of established ones.

name: LONG THIN HOUSE
problem: A building's configuration significantly influences the balance between privacy and crowding, which critically impacts occupant comfort and wellbeing.
solution: For smaller structures, avoid clustering rooms together; instead, arrange them sequentially to maximize the distance between each room. This can be achieved horizontally—creating a thin, elongated rectangular floor plan—or vertically—developing a tall, slender tower. Either approach allows for surprisingly narrow dimensions while remaining functional, with widths of 8, 10, or 12 feet being entirely viable.

name: MAIN ENTRANCE
problem: The location of a building's main entrance(s) represents perhaps the most crucial decision in the development of its floor plan.
solution: Situate the building's main entrance where it can be readily observed from primary approach routes, and give it a distinctive, prominent form that projects outward from the building façade.

name: HALF-HIDDEN GARDEN
problem: Gardens positioned too near the street lack sufficient privacy for comfortable use, while those placed too remotely feel isolated and tend to be underutilized.
solution: Avoid placing the garden entirely in front of or behind the house. Instead, position it in an intermediate location, adjacent to the house, where it is partially concealed from and partially visible to the street.

name: ENTRANCE TRANSITION
problem: Structures, particularly residential ones, that incorporate a thoughtful transition between street and interior create a more serene atmosphere than those with direct street access.
solution: Create an intermediate space between the street and front door. Guide the connecting pathway through this transitional area, marking it with various sensory shifts—changes in lighting, acoustics, direction, surface texture, elevation, enclosure (possibly using gateways), and most importantly, a change in visual perspective.

name: CAR CONNECTION
problem: Arriving at and departing from our homes are essential daily routines that often involve automobiles. However, the intersection between cars and houses is frequently marginalized and neglected, rather than being treated as the significant and aesthetically pleasing space it should be.
solution: Design the parking area and main entrance to complement each other so that the most direct path from a parked vehicle into the house—both to kitchen and living areas—always passes through the main entrance. Transform the car parking space into a well-designed room that provides an attractive and dignified place for the vehicle, rather than merely a vacant area in the landscape.

name: HIERARCHY OF OPEN SPACE
problem: When outside, individuals instinctively seek positions where their backs are protected while facing toward a more expansive area beyond their immediate surroundings.
solution: In designing any outdoor space—garden, terrace, street, park, public gathering area, or courtyard—ensure two key elements. First, create at least one smaller space that overlooks it and serves as a natural backdrop. Second, position the space and its openings to provide views of at least one larger, more distant area. This arrangement gives every outdoor space a natural "back," allowing anyone who positions themselves against this backdrop to face outward toward a more expansive vista.

name: COURTYARDS WHICH LIVE
problem: Contemporary building courtyards often remain unused and lifeless. Though intended to function as private open spaces for people's enjoyment, they frequently end up abandoned, filled with gravel and abstract art installations rather than human activity.
solution: Position each courtyard to provide a view of a larger open area beyond. Ensure that at least two or three doorways open from the building directly into the courtyard, with natural pathways between these doors crossing through the courtyard space. Along one edge, adjacent to a doorway, construct a roofed veranda or porch that seamlessly connects both the interior and the courtyard.

name: CASCADE OF ROOFS
problem: Buildings rarely maintain structural and social integrity unless the floors descend toward the ends of wings and the roof correspondingly forms a cascading pattern.
solution: Conceptualize the entire building or complex as a system of interconnected roofs. Position the largest, tallest, and widest roofs above the most significant portions of the building. When detailing the roof layout, arrange smaller roofs to cascade from these larger ones, creating a stable, self-supporting system that aligns with the hierarchy of social spaces beneath.

name: SHELTERING ROOF
problem: The roof fulfills a fundamental role in our existence. The most basic structures are essentially just roofs. When a roof is concealed, when its presence cannot be sensed throughout the building, or when it serves no practical purpose, people lose an essential sense of protection and shelter.
solution: Create sloped roofs or vaulted ceilings with fully visible surfaces. Extend the eaves down low, particularly near entrances and pausing points, bringing them as low as 6'0" or 6'6". Integrate the top floor of each wing directly into the roof structure so that the roof doesn't merely cover the space but entirely envelops it.

name: ROOF GARDEN
problem: In urban environments, rooftops constitute a substantial portion of the available surface area. Considering that a town's total sun-exposed area is limited, it becomes both logical and necessary to design roofs that maximize the benefits of sunlight and fresh air.
solution: Design sections of almost every roof system to function as usable roof gardens. Create flat areas, potentially terraced for plantings, with spaces for sitting and resting, including private retreats. Distribute roof gardens across various levels, and always ensure direct access to the roof garden from an inhabited portion of the building.

name: ARCADES
problem: Arcades—covered walkways at building edges that exist partially indoors and partially outdoors—serve a crucial function in facilitating human interactions with buildings.
solution: Construct arcades wherever paths run alongside buildings. Utilize these arcades primarily to connect buildings to one another, enabling pedestrians to move from place to place under the protection of these covered passages.

name: PATHS AND GOALS
problem: Path design will only feel appropriate and comfortable when it harmonizes with the act of walking—a process far more nuanced than most people realize.
solution: Begin path layout by identifying goals at natural points of interest. Then connect these goals to form pathways. The paths may be either straight or gently curved between goals, with paving that expands around each goal point. Keep goals at moderate distances, never more than a few hundred feet apart from one another.

lower_patterns.name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES

name: PATH SHAPE
problem: Modern streets primarily serve as thoroughfares rather than places where people can linger and gather, contrary to their potential social function.
solution: Design public paths with a widened central area and narrower endpoints, creating an enclosed space that invites people to stay rather than merely pass through.

name: BUILDING FRONTS
problem: Building set-backs, originally implemented to ensure public welfare through adequate light and air for all structures, have ironically contributed significantly to the decline of streets as vibrant social environments.
solution: Never permit set-backs between streets, paths, or public land and the buildings that face them. These set-backs provide no valuable function and typically diminish the quality of open areas between buildings. Construct buildings directly adjacent to pathways; work to change regulations in communities where outdated building codes prevent this approach. Allow building frontages to adopt slightly irregular angles as they adapt to street contours.

name: PEDESTRIAN DENSITY
problem: Many contemporary public squares intended to be bustling plazas are instead abandoned and lifeless.
solution: For public squares, courtyards, pedestrian streets, and other gathering places, calculate the average number of people present at any given moment (P). Size the area between 150P and 300P square feet to achieve appropriate pedestrian density.

name: ACTIVITY POCKETS
problem: The vitality of a public square naturally develops around its perimeter. If this edge fails to function properly, the space never becomes animated with human activity.
solution: Encircle public gathering spaces with activity pockets—small, partially enclosed areas at the edges that extend into the open space between paths. These pockets should contain functions that naturally encourage people to pause and engage with the environment.

name: STAIR SEATS
problem: In active spaces, the most appealing spots are those elevated enough to provide perspective but low enough to keep people engaged with the activity.
solution: In public spaces where people tend to linger, incorporate a few steps at edges where staircases descend or where level changes occur. Make these elevated areas easily accessible from below, creating spaces where people can gather and sit to observe ongoing activities.

name: SOMETHING ROUGHLY IN THE MIDDLE
problem: A public space lacking a central element is likely to remain unused and empty.
solution: Among the natural pathways crossing a public square, courtyard, or common area, select an element to position approximately in the middle—such as a fountain, tree, statue, clock tower with seating, windmill, or bandstand. Choose something that provides strong visual interest to the square, drawing people toward the center. Position it naturally between paths rather than forcing it into the exact geometric center.

name: INTIMACY GRADIENT
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: When spaces in a building aren't organized in a sequence that reflects their degrees of privacy, visits from strangers, friends, guests, clients, and family members will inevitably feel somewhat uncomfortable and awkward.
solution: Arrange the spaces within a building to create a progression beginning with the entrance and most public areas, then transitioning through increasingly private zones, and culminating in the most intimate and private spaces.

name: INDOOR SUNLIGHT
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: When appropriate rooms have southern exposure, homes feel bright, cheerful and welcoming; when inappropriate rooms face south, homes can feel dark and dreary.
solution:
Position the most significant rooms along the southern edge of the building, and extend the structure along the east-west axis.
Adjust the layout to ensure appropriate rooms receive southeastern and southwestern sunlight. For instance: give common spaces full southern exposure, place bedrooms to the southeast, and position the porch to the southwest. For most climate zones, this requires a building shape that is elongated on the east-west axis.

name: COMMON AREAS AT THE HEART
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: Social groups - whether families, work teams, or school communities - cannot thrive without regular informal interactions among members.
solution: Establish a single shared space for each social group. Position it at the center of gravity of all spaces the group uses, and arrange it so that paths entering and exiting the building run tangentially to this central area.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: ENTRANCE ROOM
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: When entering or exiting a building, people need a transitional space that serves as a passage both inside and outside the structure. This space functions as the entrance room.
solution: Create a light-filled room at the main entrance that bridges indoor and outdoor spaces, encompassing area both outside and inside the building. The exterior portion might resemble a traditional porch, while the interior could function as a hall or sitting area.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: THE FLOW THROUGH ROOMS
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: Movement between rooms is equally important as the rooms themselves, and how this movement is arranged significantly impacts social interaction within those rooms.
solution: Minimize corridors and passages whenever possible. Instead, utilize public and common rooms as spaces for both movement and gathering. Arrange common rooms in a chain or loop pattern, enabling movement from room to room, with private rooms opening directly into these public spaces. In all cases, create generous indoor circulation paths that form wide, ample loops throughout the house, featuring views of fireplaces and large windows.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: SHORT PASSAGES
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: "... long sterile corridors set the scene for everything bad about modern architecture."
solution: Design passages to be brief. Make them as room-like as possible by incorporating carpets or wood flooring, furniture, bookshelves, and attractive windows. Create generous shapes and always provide abundant light; the most effective corridors and passages have windows extending along an entire wall.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: STAIRCASE AS A STAGE
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: A staircase serves more than just connecting different floors. It constitutes its own space, volume, and integral part of the building; if this space isn't enlivened, it becomes a dead zone that fragments the building and disrupts its functional processes.
solution: Situate the main staircase in a prominent, central, and visible location. Design the entire staircase as a room (or as a courtyard if it's exterior). Integrate the stair with the room so they function as one unit, with the staircase descending around one or two walls of the room. Design the bottom of the stair to widen out with open windows or balustrades and broad steps, allowing people descending to participate in the room's activities while on the stairs, and encouraging those below to use the stairs as seating.

name: ZEN VIEW
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: The classic zen view concept originates from a renowned Japanese house that gives this pattern its name.
solution:
When presented with a beautiful view, resist the temptation to install large windows that constantly expose it. Instead, position view windows at transition points – along pathways, in hallways, entryways, staircases, or between rooms.
