When properly placed, these windows offer glimpses of distant vistas as people approach or pass by them, but the view remains hidden from areas where people typically linger.

name: TAPESTRY OF LIGHT AND DARK
section: Buildings: Gradients of space and movement
problem: Buildings with uniform lighting lack distinct "places" that effectively frame human activities. This occurs primarily because effective settings are largely defined by their lighting conditions.
solution: Design alternating areas of light and darkness throughout the building, arranging them so people naturally gravitate toward light when approaching important spaces: seating areas, entrances, staircases, passages, and areas of special beauty. Make other areas deliberately darker to enhance this contrast.

name: COUPLE'S REALM
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Children in a family often disrupt the intimacy and private connection that partners need to maintain with each other.
solution: Designate a specific area of the home separate from common spaces and children's rooms where the partners can enjoy privacy together. Create a quick access path to the children's rooms, but ensure this area remains clearly distinct as a separate domain.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: CHILDREN'S REALM
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Children need space to release their considerable energy when necessary, or they will create frustration for themselves and everyone else in the family.
solution: Begin by establishing a small area exclusively for children - specifically their bed cluster. Place this toward the back of the house, positioned to allow for a continuous play space extending from this cluster to the street, creating a wide pathway through the house. This path may be messy with toys scattered along it, connecting to essential rooms children need - primarily the bathroom and kitchen - passing alongside the common area (while keeping quiet sitting spaces and the couple's realm completely separate and protected), and extending to the street either through its own entrance or through the main entrance room. End this pathway in a sheltered outdoor room connected to the street, spacious enough for children to play in during rainy weather while still being outdoors.

name: SLEEPING TO THE EAST
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: This pattern frequently generates disagreement. Nevertheless, we stand by our conviction on this matter.
solution: Orient sleeping areas toward the east so occupants naturally awaken with the morning light. This generally means positioning bedrooms on the eastern portion of the house, though western placements can work if they include an eastern courtyard or terrace adjacent to the sleeping space.

name: FARMHOUSE KITCHEN
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: The concept of a kitchen as a segregated, utilitarian food factory stems from an era of household servants and was later perpetuated when women assumed this servile role.
solution: Design a kitchen larger than standard, incorporating the "family room" function, and position it centrally within the common areas rather than isolated at the back of the house. Make it spacious enough to accommodate a substantial table with chairs—both comfortable and functional—while arranging counters, stove, and sink around the perimeter. Ensure the space is well-lit and inviting.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: PRIVATE TERRACE ON THE STREET
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Houses often have an ambiguous relationship with the street: they either expose themselves completely, sacrificing privacy, or they disconnect entirely, losing connection with community life.
solution: Design common areas to open onto a spacious terrace or porch overlooking the street. Elevate this terrace slightly above street level and include a low boundary wall—high enough to provide privacy when seated, but low enough to maintain visual connection with passersby when desired, while preventing direct views into the home's interior spaces.

name: A ROOM OF ONE'S OWN
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Meaningful connection with others requires balancing with regular opportunities for solitude.
solution: Provide each family member with a personal space, particularly adults. This could range from a minimal setup—an alcove with desk, shelving, and a privacy curtain—to a full cottage, such as a TEENAGER'S COTTAGE (154) or OLD AGE COTTAGE (155). In all instances, especially for adults, place these spaces at the extreme end of the intimacy gradient, distant from communal areas.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: SEQUENCE OF SITTING SPACES
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Every building corner offers potential for a sitting area. However, each requires different levels of comfort and enclosure based on its position along the intimacy gradient.
solution: Create a progression of sitting spaces throughout the building with varying degrees of enclosure. Place the most formal ones in dedicated rooms with complete enclosure; situate the most casual in corners of other rooms without screening; and position intermediate spaces with partial boundaries that maintain connection to larger areas while providing some separation.

name: BED CLUSTER
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Each child needs personal space centered around their bed. Yet across many, perhaps all, cultures, young children experience isolation when sleeping alone in overly private areas.
solution: Arrange children's beds in alcoves or small alcove-like spaces surrounding a shared play area. Design each alcove generously enough to include additional elements—a table, chair, or shelving—providing space for personal belongings. Use curtains rather than walls or doors to connect these spaces to the common area, avoiding excessive isolation of the sleeping spaces.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: BATHING ROOM
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: "The motions we call bathing are mere ablutions which formerly preceded the bath. The place where they are performed, though adequate for the routine, does not deserve to be called a bathroom." Bernard Rudofsky
solution:
Consolidate bathing facilities—toilet, shower, and basins—into a unified tiled area. Position this bathing room adjacent to the couple's realm with private access, halfway between private and communal areas of the house; if possible, provide outdoor access via a small balcony or enclosed garden.
Install a generous bath large enough for at least two people to fully immerse; include an efficient shower and basins for practical cleaning needs; and incorporate two or three strategically placed towel racks—near the door, shower, and sink.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: BULK STORAGE
section: Buildings: Important area or rooms in the house
problem: Every home and workplace requires space for storing bulky items—suitcases, unused furniture, archived files, boxes—things not regularly used but not yet ready to discard.
solution: Incorporate bulk storage from the beginning of your design rather than treating it as an afterthought. Allocate at least 15 to 20 percent of the total building area for this purpose. Locate this storage in a section of the building where construction costs are lower than other rooms, as it doesn't require finishing details.

name: FLEXIBLE OFFICE SPACE
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: Can we create a workspace specifically designed for productivity while remaining adaptable to infinite configurations and arrangements?
solution:
Design office spaces as wings of open area with freestanding columns along the perimeters to define semi-private and common spaces that flow into one another. Install sufficient columns to allow various configurations over time, always maintaining a semi-permanent character.
If you're designing for a known working group, create a more home-like environment tailored to their specific needs. In either scenario, incorporate diverse spatial qualities throughout the office—comparable to the varied room types found in a traditional large house.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: COMMUNAL EATING
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: No human community can maintain cohesion without shared meals.
solution: Establish a dedicated space for communal dining in every institution and social group. Make shared meals a regular ritual. Specifically, implement a communal lunch in every workplace—a genuine meal around a shared table (not from containers, vending machines, or bags)—as an important, comfortable daily event with accommodation for guests. In our Center work group, we found the most beautiful arrangement was taking turns cooking lunch. The meal became an event: a gathering that each member invested with love and energy on their day to prepare.

name: SMALL WORK GROUPS
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: When a workspace accommodates more than six people, it becomes essential to avoid forcing everyone into a single undifferentiated space, instead allowing for subdivision into smaller functional groups.
solution: Segment organizations into small, spatially distinct work units with fewer than six people each. Configure these groups so each member maintains partial visibility of their colleagues, while arranging multiple groups to share common resources—entrance, food, office equipment, water fountains, and restrooms.

name: RECEPTION WELCOMES YOU
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: Have you experienced entering a public building only to be processed impersonally by the reception desk, as if you were a package?
solution: Create a welcoming environment just inside the entrance featuring comfortable seating, a fireplace, and refreshments. Position the reception desk not as a barrier between the receptionist and the welcome area, but offset at an angle—allowing the staff member to rise, approach visitors personally, and invite them to sit comfortably.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: A PLACE TO WAIT
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: Waiting inherently contains conflicting psychological demands.
solution: Transform waiting areas (bus stops, appointment lobbies, airport gates) into positive experiences by blending waiting with engaging activities—newspapers, coffee, games—that attract even non-waiting visitors. Alternatively, design spaces that guide those waiting into peaceful contemplation through quietude and positive silence.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: SMALL MEETING ROOMS
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: Effectiveness diminishes as meeting size increases, yet organizations typically invest their resources and focus on large conference rooms and auditoriums.
solution: Ensure at least 70 percent of meeting spaces are compact - accommodating 11 people or fewer. Distribute these rooms evenly throughout the building's public areas, interspersed among workspaces.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: HALF-PRIVATE OFFICE
section: Buildings: Important areas or rooms in the office
problem: How can we achieve optimal equilibrium between isolation and collaboration in office environments?
solution: Steer clear of entirely enclosed or isolated offices. Design each workspace, whether for individuals or small groups of two or three, to be partially open to neighboring work areas and the surrounding environment. Create comfortable seating near the entrance, while positioning the actual work area deeper inside, away from the doorway.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: ROOMS TO RENT
problem: Space requirements within buildings fluctuate cyclically as circumstances evolve. Buildings need to be adaptable to these unpredictable expansions and contractions in spatial needs.
solution: Design at least a portion of the building to be rentable: provide it with a separate entrance in addition to its normal connection to the house. Ensure the main entrance can be easily sealed off without disrupting the house's circulation patterns, and make certain a bathroom is directly accessible from this room without requiring passage through the main residence.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: TEENAGER'S COTTAGE
problem: When a teenager's living space fails to acknowledge their growing need for autonomy, family conflict becomes inevitable.
solution: To acknowledge a child's transition toward adulthood, convert their space within the home into a cottage-like area that physically represents their emerging independence. Keep this cottage connected to the main house, but design it as a clearly distinct extension, positioned far from the parents' bedroom, featuring its own private entrance and possibly its own roof.

name: OLD AGE COTTAGE
problem: Elderly individuals, particularly those living alone, face a challenging paradox. Forces inevitably push them toward greater independence: children relocate, neighborhoods transform, and loved ones pass away. Yet simultaneously, aging naturally increases their reliance on basic conveniences and social connections to the surrounding community.
solution: Construct compact cottages specifically designed for elderly residents. Build some on the property of larger homes for grandparents; construct others on separate, smaller-than-standard lots. In all instances, position these cottages at ground level, directly adjacent to the street where pedestrians pass by, and in close proximity to neighborhood amenities and shared spaces.

name: SETTLED WORK
problem: A sense of established work provides essential mental tranquility in later years. However, our culture undermines this experience by creating divisions between career and retirement, and between workplace and residence.
solution: Provide each person, particularly as they age, the opportunity to establish a personal workspace within or very near their home. Create an environment that can evolve gradually, perhaps beginning as a weekend hobby space and eventually developing into a fully-equipped, productive, and comfortable workshop.

name: HOME WORKSHOP
problem: As work becomes increasingly decentralized, the importance of home-based workshops continues to grow significantly.
solution: Create a dedicated space within the home for meaningful work - not merely a hobby area, but a genuine workplace. Modify zoning regulations to support modest, low-impact work operations in residential neighborhoods. Allocate approximately a few hundred square feet for the workshop and position it to be visible from the street, allowing the owner to display a business sign.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: OPEN STAIRS
problem: Interior staircases severely limit the connection between upper floors and street life, potentially causing significant social disconnection.
solution: Minimize internal staircases in institutional buildings whenever possible. Link all independent households, community services, and work groups on upper floors directly to ground level. Accomplish this by installing open staircases that are accessible directly from the street. Whether covered or uncovered depending on climate, ensure the stairway remains open at ground level without doors, effectively making it an extension of the street. Avoid constructing upper-floor corridors. Instead, create open landings or an open arcade where multiple upper units share a single staircase.

name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM
problem: Given options, individuals consistently prefer rooms illuminated from multiple sides, leaving spaces lit from just one direction largely unused and vacant.
solution: Position each room so it has exterior space on at least two sides, then install windows in these exterior walls to allow daylight to enter every room from multiple directions.

name: BUILDING EDGE
problem: Buildings are typically conceived as inward-focused structures centered around their internal spaces. The outward orientation of buildings is frequently overlooked in design considerations.
solution: Consider the building's edge as a substantive element with volume rather than a mere line or interface without depth. Create indentations along the building perimeter with areas that encourage people to pause. Develop spaces with depth and coverage - places to sit, lean, and stroll - especially at points along the perimeter that overlook engaging outdoor activities.

higher_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: SUNNY PLACE
problem: The southern-facing area immediately outside a building - that intersection between walls and ground where sunlight falls - needs deliberate development to create a space where people can enjoy the sunshine.
solution: Within a south-facing courtyard, garden, or yard, identify the location between the building and exterior that receives optimal sunlight. Develop this spot into a special sunny retreat - make it the primary outdoor space, suitable for working in the sun, installing a swing and special plantings, or sunbathing. Take particular care to position the sunny area where it's protected from wind. Even consistent mild breezes will prevent enjoyment of the most appealing location.

name: NORTH FACE
problem: Examine the northern sides of buildings in your area. You'll typically find these areas are lifeless, damp, gloomy and serve little purpose. Despite this, towns contain hundreds of acres on buildings' north sides, and it's unavoidable that land will always exist in this position wherever buildings are constructed.
solution: Design the building's north face as a sloping cascade that extends down to ground level, allowing sunlight that would normally create long northern shadows to reach the ground immediately adjacent to the building.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: OUTDOOR ROOM
problem: Traditional gardens provide space for activities like lying in grass, swinging, playing croquet, growing flowers, or playing fetch with a dog. However, there's another way to experience the outdoors that gardens don't adequately address.
solution: Create an outdoor space with sufficient enclosure that it feels like a room despite being open to the sky. Define its corners with columns, consider partial roofing with a trellis or adjustable canvas, and establish "walls" using elements such as fences, sitting walls, screens, hedges, or the building's exterior walls.

name: STREET WINDOWS
problem: Streets without windows create a blind and frightening environment. Similarly, it's uncomfortable to be in a house that borders a public street but has no windows facing that street.
solution: For buildings adjacent to busy streets, incorporate windows with window seats overlooking the street. Position these at locations like bedrooms or along passages and stairs where people frequently pass by. For first-floor windows, maintain sufficient height to ensure privacy.

name: OPENING TO THE STREET
problem: Witnessing activity motivates action. When people can view into spaces from the street, their world expands and becomes more enriched, fostering greater understanding and creating opportunities for communication and learning.
solution:
For any public space that relies on street exposure for its success, open it up with a fully opening wall that can be thrown wide open. If possible, extend part of the activity across the pedestrian path so it straddles the walkway, allowing pedestrians to walk through the activity as they move along the path.
There are dozens of ways to build such an opening. For example, a wall can be made very cheaply with a simple plywood hanging shutter sliding on an overhead rail, which can be removed to open up completely, and locked in place at night.

name: GALLERY SURROUND
problem: When occupants cannot exit a building onto balconies and terraces that overlook the surrounding outdoor space, neither they nor people outside have any medium helping them feel that the building and the broader public world are interconnected.
solution: At every level of the building where possible, construct elements such as porches, galleries, arcades, balconies, niches, outdoor seats, awnings, and trellised rooms along the building edges—particularly where they open to public spaces and streets—and connect them directly to interior rooms via doorways.

name: SIX-FOOT BALCONY
problem: Balconies and porches that are less than six feet in depth rarely get used.
solution: When constructing a balcony, porch, gallery, or terrace, always ensure it's at least six feet deep. If feasible, recess at least a portion of it into the building rather than having it completely cantilevered out and separated from the building by a simple line, and include partial enclosure.

name: CONNECTION TO THE EARTH
problem: A house can feel disconnected from the surrounding natural environment unless its floors directly integrate with the earth around the structure.
solution: Link the building to its surrounding earth by creating a network of paths, terraces, and steps around its perimeter. Place these elements deliberately to create ambiguous boundaries—making it difficult to determine precisely where the building ends and the earth begins.

lower_patterns.name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES

name: TERRACED SLOPE
problem: On sloped terrain, runoff-caused erosion can damage soil. This also creates uneven rainwater distribution across the land, which is less beneficial for plant growth than evenly distributed moisture would be.
solution:
On all sloping land—whether in fields, parks, public gardens, or private residential gardens—create a system of terraces and bunds that follow the contour lines. Construct these by building low walls along the contour lines and backfilling with earth to form terraces.
There is no reason why the building itself should fit into the terraces - it can comfortably cross terrace lines.

name: FRUIT TREES
problem: In climates suitable for fruit trees, orchards provide the landscape with a distinctive, almost magical character—consider Southern California's orange groves, Japan's cherry trees, or Greece's olive trees. Unfortunately, urban expansion typically destroys these trees and their special qualities.
solution: Create small fruit tree orchards in gardens and on common land alongside paths and streets, in parks, and throughout neighborhoods—particularly in locations where established groups can maintain the trees and harvest their fruit.

name: TREE PLACES
problem: When trees are planted or trimmed without consideration for the unique spaces they can create, they become essentially useless to the people who need them.
solution: When planting trees, arrange them according to their natural characteristics to form enclosures, avenues, squares, groves, and individual spreading specimens toward the center of open areas. Shape nearby buildings in response to the trees, ensuring that both the trees individually and the trees and buildings together create functional spaces for people.

name: GARDEN GROWING WILD
problem: A garden that develops according to its own natural patterns isn't completely wild, yet it's not entirely artificial either.
solution: Cultivate grasses, mosses, bushes, flowers, and trees in a manner resembling their natural occurrence: mixed together, without barriers between different plantings, eliminating bare soil, avoiding formal flower beds, and creating all boundaries and edges with rough stone, brick, and wood that become integrated with the natural growth.

name: GARDEN WALL
problem: Gardens and small public parks fail to provide adequate relief from noise without proper protection.
solution: Create some form of enclosure to shield a quiet garden's interior from traffic sights and sounds. For large gardens or parks, this enclosure can be softer, incorporating elements like bushes, trees, and slopes. However, smaller gardens require harder, more defined enclosures—the smaller the garden, the more substantial the barrier needed. For very small gardens, form the enclosure using buildings or walls, as hedges and fences alone won't sufficiently block sound.

name: TRELLISED WALK
problem: Trellised walkways possess a distinctive beauty. They're so unique and different from other path designs that they're almost archetypal in nature.
solution: For paths requiring special protection or intimacy, construct a trellis over the walkway and plant climbing flowers on it. Use the trellis to help define and shape the outdoor spaces on both sides of the path.

lower_patterns.name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES

name: GREENHOUSE
problem: While various technologies attempt to convert solar energy into hot water or electricity, we often overlook the simplest and most traditional method: capturing solar heat within a greenhouse to cultivate flowers and vegetables.
solution: For moderate climates, integrate a greenhouse with your home or workplace, ensuring it functions both as an accessible interior room connected directly to the house and as an extension of the garden that can be entered directly from outside.

name: GARDEN SEAT
problem: Every garden needs at least one tranquil spot, a secluded garden seat, where an individual or a pair can retreat to connect with themselves and experience nature without distractions.
solution: Create a secluded area within your garden featuring a comfortable seat surrounded by dense plantings and sunlight. Select the location with care, prioritizing the spot that offers the most profound sense of seclusion and solitude.

name: VEGETABLE GARDEN
problem: Growing vegetables should be accessible to all families in a healthy community. This isn't merely a pastime for enthusiasts but an essential component of human existence.
solution: Designate a portion of land in your private garden or on community property specifically for growing vegetables. Each family of four requires approximately one-tenth of an acre. Ensure the vegetable garden is positioned in a sunny, central location accessible to all households it serves. Surround it with fencing and construct a small shed nearby to store gardening tools.

name: COMPOST
problem: Contemporary sewage disposal methods contaminate major natural water bodies while depriving the surrounding land of essential nutrients.
solution: Position all toilets above a dry composting chamber. Direct organic waste chutes to this same chamber and utilize the resulting combined materials as fertilizer.

name: ALCOVES
problem: A uniform room with consistent height cannot adequately serve a group. To foster togetherness, a space must simultaneously offer opportunities for solitude, allowing individuals or pairs to find privacy within the shared area.
solution: Create compact spaces along the perimeter of any common room, typically no wider than 6 feet and between 3 to 6 feet in depth, though potentially smaller. These alcoves should accommodate two people for conversation or activities, and occasionally be large enough to fit a desk or table.

name: WINDOW PLACE
problem: People have a natural affinity for window seats, bay windows, and spacious windows with low sills complemented by comfortable chairs positioned nearby.
solution: Transform at least one window into a dedicated "window place" in every room where you spend significant daytime hours.

higher_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: THE FIRE
problem: Fire has no adequate replacement in our lives.
solution: Establish a fire in a communal area—perhaps the kitchen—to create a natural focal point for conversation, reflection, and imagination. Position it strategically to connect the surrounding social spaces, allowing each area a glimpse of the flames. Install a window or alternative focal element to maintain the area's appeal when the fire isn't burning.

name: EATING ATMOSPHERE
problem: When dining together, people may be genuinely connected in spirit or emotionally distant. Some spaces encourage leisurely, comfortable eating and togetherness, while others prompt quick meals followed by retreating elsewhere for relaxation.
solution: Position a substantial table in the eating area's center, sized appropriately for the entire family or group. Install lighting directly above to create a focused pool of light, and define the space with walls or contrasting darkness. Ensure the area is spacious enough for comfortable chair movement, and incorporate nearby shelves and counters for dining-related items.

name: WORKSPACE ENCLOSURE
problem: Workspaces that are excessively enclosed or exposed hinder effective work. An optimal workspace achieves the right balance between these extremes.
solution: Allocate at least 60 square feet for each workspace. Construct walls and windows around each area so that their combined area (counting windows at half-value) constitutes 50-75 percent of what would exist if all four walls enclosing the 60 square feet were solid. Keep at least 5 feet of open space at the front, leading into a larger area. Position the desk to provide the worker with a view, either forward or to the side. If others work nearby, arrange the enclosure to create a sense of connection with two or three colleagues, but never place more than eight workspaces within visual or audible range of each other.

name: COOKING LAYOUT
problem: Kitchen counters that are either too abbreviated or too extensive make cooking uncomfortable.
solution:
To find the optimal balance between an undersized kitchen and one that's too spread out, arrange the stove, sink, food storage, and counter so that:
1. No two of the four are more than 10 feet apart.
2. The total length of counter - excluding sink, stove, and refrigerator - is at least 12 feet.
3. No one section of the counter is less than 4 feet long.
There is no need for the counter to be continuous or entirely "built-in" as it is in many modern kitchens - it can even consist of free-standing tables or counter tops. Only the three functional relationships described above are critical.

name: SITTING CIRCLE
problem: Seating arrangements—whether chairs grouped together, a sofa with a chair, or cushions arranged on the floor—are fundamental elements of our daily environments. Yet creating configurations that foster animation and vitality rather than sterility is remarkably nuanced. Most arrangements remain unused, with people avoiding them and no activity occurring there. Others seem to naturally gather and energize life around them. What distinguishes these two outcomes?
solution: Position each sitting space in a protected location, away from thoroughfares and movement, with a roughly circular arrangement. Design the room to subtly suggest this circular formation—not too rigidly—with paths and activities surrounding it to naturally draw people toward the seating when they feel inclined to sit. Arrange chairs and cushions in a loose circle, providing slightly more seating than necessary.

name: COMMUNAL SLEEPING
problem: Sleep is practiced as a communal activity in many traditional societies, without the sexual connotations prevalent in contemporary Western culture. We believe this may serve a vital social function, potentially as fundamental and necessary to human well-being as communal eating.
solution:
Design sleeping areas that allow children and adults to occasionally share the same space, within sight and sound of one another, as an alternative to their usual sleeping arrangements.
This can be done in the common area near the fireplace, where the entire household and guests can sleep together - one large mat and some blankets in an alcove. It is also possible to build bed alcoves for overnight guests, in an extended couple's realm.

name: MARRIAGE BED
problem: The bed represents the focal point of a couple's shared life—a place for lying together, conversation, intimacy, sleep, leisurely mornings, and mutual care during illness. However, beds and bedrooms are rarely designed to enhance these meaningful experiences, preventing them from fully manifesting.
solution: At an appropriate stage in a couple's relationship, they should create a distinctive bed that serves as an intimate anchor for their lives together. This might feature a slight enclosure with a low ceiling or canopy, with the room shaped around it—perhaps a small space built specifically for the bed with numerous windows. Give the bed a unique form, possibly as a four-poster with a headboard that can be hand-carved or painted over time.

name: BED ALCOVE
problem: The concept of bedrooms as we know them lacks coherent purpose and function.
solution: When constructing a compact dwelling around 300-400 square feet—perhaps with plans for future expansion—this pattern becomes crucial. In such cases, placing the alcoves adjacent to the family room will likely be most effective.

name: DRESSING ROOM
problem: Activities related to clothing—changing, storing, and managing garments—are isolated tasks that don't naturally integrate with other household activities. These self-contained functions actually disrupt other activities and require dedicated space with a singular purpose.
solution: Provide each person with a dressing room—whether individual or shared—positioned between their sleeping area and bathroom. Design this space with sufficient dimensions to include: a clear area at least six feet in diameter, approximately six linear feet of clothing hanging space, another six feet of open shelving, two to three drawers, and a mirror.

lower_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: CEILING HEIGHT VARIETY
problem: A structure with uniform ceiling heights throughout fails to create environments where people feel truly at ease.
solution: Implement continuous variation in ceiling heights across the building, particularly between connecting rooms, to establish a sense of the relative intimacy in different spaces. Specifically, design higher ceilings for public areas or rooms intended for large gatherings (10-12 feet), medium heights for spaces accommodating smaller groups (7-9 feet), and very low ceilings for intimate areas designed for just one or two people (6-7 feet).

name: THE SHAPE OF INDOOR SPACE
problem: The flawless geometric squares and rectangles characteristic of ultra-modern architecture lack meaningful connection to human needs or structural logic. They merely represent rigid ideals and fantasies that emerge when people become overly focused on systems and production methods.
solution: With some exceptions, design each indoor space or section of a space as an approximate rectangle, featuring relatively straight walls, near-right-angle corners, and a generally symmetrical vault covering each room.

name: WINDOWS OVERLOOKING LIFE
problem: Rooms that lack views create prison-like environments for their occupants.
solution: Position windows in each room so their combined area roughly meets regional recommendations (at least 25% of floor area in the San Francisco Bay Area), and place them to maximize views of external activity—whether street life, peaceful gardens, or any scene that contrasts with the interior environment.

higher_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: HALF-OPEN WALL
problem: Rooms that are excessively enclosed inhibit the natural progression of social interactions and transitions between different social moments. Conversely, spaces that are too open cannot support the distinct events that social life demands.
solution: Fine-tune the arrangement of walls, openings, and windows in each indoor space until you achieve the optimal balance between open, flowing areas and enclosed, cell-like spaces. Don't automatically assume each space must be a complete room, nor that all spaces should merge together. The ideal balance always exists somewhere between these extremes—no room completely enclosed, yet no space entirely connected to another. Employ various elements such as columns, partially open walls, porches, interior windows, sliding doors, low sills, French doors, and sitting walls to achieve this balance.

name: INTERIOR WINDOWS
problem: While windows typically connect indoor and outdoor environments, there are many situations where an indoor space would benefit from a visual connection to another interior area.
solution: Install fully glazed, fixed windows between rooms that tend to feel lifeless due to insufficient activity or between interior spaces that suffer from inadequate natural light.

name: STAIRCASE VOLUME
problem: This pattern is included because our research has shown that non-professionals frequently miscalculate the volume required for staircases, resulting in plans that cannot be implemented.
solution: Create a two-story volume to house the staircase, whether it's straight, L-shaped, U-shaped, or C-shaped. The stairs may range from 2 feet wide (for very steep stairs) to 5 feet wide (for generous, gradual stairs). In all cases, the entire stairwell must constitute one complete structural bay with two-story height. Don't assume all stairs must conform to the "standard" 30-degree angle. The steepest stairs might almost resemble ladders, while the most generous can be as gradual as ramps and quite wide. When determining the exact slope of your stairs, remember the relationship: riser + tread = 17½ inches.

name: CORNER DOORS
problem: A room's effectiveness is largely determined by door placement. If doors create movement patterns that disrupt the functional spaces within a room, occupants will never feel completely comfortable.
solution: Except in spacious rooms, placing a door in the middle of a wall is rarely optimal. This arrangement works in entrance rooms, where the door defines the room's character. However, in most spaces, particularly smaller ones, position doors as close to the corners as possible. In rooms with two doors that serve as passageways, keep both doors at the same end of the room.

name: THICK WALLS
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Dwellings with thin, smooth walls made from prefabricated panels, concrete, gypsum, steel, aluminum, or glass inevitably remain impersonal and lifeless.
solution: Consider the possibility that your building's walls can have substantial thickness, occupying significant volume—even usable space—rather than functioning as mere thin membranes without depth. Identify the locations where these thick walls would be most appropriate.

name: CLOSETS BETWEEN ROOMS
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Storage and closet planning is typically addressed as an afterthought in architectural design.
solution: Identify all rooms requiring closets. Then position the closets along interior walls that separate two rooms or that divide rooms from passages where sound insulation is needed. Arrange them to create transition zones for doorways into rooms. Never place closets against exterior walls, as this wastes opportunities for effective acoustic insulation and blocks valuable natural light.

name: SUNNY COUNTER
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Dark, gloomy kitchens create depressing environments. Kitchens actually need more sunlight than other rooms, not less.
solution: Install the main section of the kitchen counter on the south and southeast sides of the kitchen, surrounded by large windows that allow sunlight to stream in and fill the kitchen with warm, yellow light during both morning and afternoon hours.

name: OPEN SHELVES
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Excessively deep cabinets waste valuable space and create frustration when needed items are hidden behind other objects.
solution: Line walls with narrow shelving of various depths, but always shallow enough that items can be arranged in a single layer with nothing concealed behind anything else.

name: WAIST-HIGH SHELF
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Every home and workplace has a daily "traffic" of frequently handled objects. When these items aren't readily accessible, daily life becomes awkward and error-prone, with things being forgotten or misplaced.
solution: Install waist-high shelves around at least part of the main living and working areas. Make these shelves long, 9-15 inches deep, with storage space or cabinets underneath. Break the continuity of the shelf to accommodate seating, windows, and doorways.

name: BUILT-IN SEATS
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Built-in seating offers comfort and luxury to buildings, but frequently fails in practice. Common issues include improper placement, insufficient width, incorrect back angle, poor viewing position, or uncomfortable hardness. This pattern provides guidance for creating truly functional built-in seats.
solution: Test the location before construction by placing an existing armchair or sofa in your intended position. Adjust it until you find an optimal spot. Leave it there for several days to verify you genuinely enjoy sitting there. If you find yourself naturally gravitating to sit in it, you've found the right location. Then construct your built-in seat with identical width and cushioning to ensure its success.

name: CHILD CAVES
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: Children love to be in tiny, cave-like places.
solution: Create small "caves" in all areas where children play - around the house, in neighborhoods, and schools. Utilize natural leftover spaces such as areas under staircases or beneath kitchen counters. Keep these spaces intimate with low ceiling heights between 2'6" and 4', and design entrances to be small and cozy.

name: SECRET PLACE
section: Buildings: Give depth to the walls
problem: How can architecture accommodate our innate desires for concealment, hiding, and the discovery of precious hidden things?
solution: Incorporate a small, locked and secret space within the home - perhaps just a few square feet in size. Design it to be virtually undiscoverable until someone reveals its location. This space can serve as storage for household archives or other significant secrets.

name: STRUCTURE FOLLOWS SOCIAL SPACES
problem: Buildings feel uncomfortable to occupants when the physical architecture (defined by columns, walls, and ceilings) doesn't align with the social spaces (defined by activities and human interactions).
solution: Establish this fundamental construction principle: never allow engineering requirements to dictate the building's form. Instead, position all load-bearing elements - columns, walls, and floors - to complement the social spaces within the building. The engineering structure should adapt to support human activities, rather than forcing social spaces to conform to structural necessities.

name: EFFICIENT STRUCTURE
problem: Buildings employ various structural systems: column and beam, load-bearing walls with slab floors, vaults, domes, or tents. But which approach or combination is truly most efficient? What's the optimal way to distribute building materials to create strong, well-enclosed spaces while minimizing material usage?
solution: Design the building as a unified body of compressive material. Geometrically, envision it as a three-dimensional system of individually vaulted spaces, predominantly rectangular. Use thin load-bearing walls stiffened with intermittent columns. Reinforce wall junctions, connections between walls and vaults, and areas surrounding openings with additional thickness.

name: GOOD MATERIALS
problem: Modern industrial society presents a fundamental conflict regarding building materials.
solution: Select only biodegradable materials with low energy requirements that can be easily modified on-site. For primary structural elements, consider ultra-lightweight concrete (40-60 lbs) and earth-based materials such as tamped earth, brick, and tile. For secondary components, utilize wood planks, gypsum, plywood, cloth, chickenwire, paper, cardboard, particle board, corrugated iron, lime plasters, bamboo, rope, and tile.

name: GRADUAL STIFFENING
problem: Pattern languages are fundamentally about creating buildings uniquely adapted to individual needs and sites. This philosophy requires building plans that remain loose and fluid to accommodate subtle adaptations.
solution: Understand that construction isn't like assembling an erector set from discrete components. Instead, think of it as weaving a structure that begins globally complete but flexible, then progressively increases in stiffness while maintaining some flexibility, before finally achieving complete rigidity and strength. In contemporary building, this approach naturally translates to erecting a shell of sheet materials, then strengthening it by adding compressive fill.

name: ROOF LAYOUT
problem: What roof design will organically integrate with your building's nature?
solution: Design your roof plan so each distinct roof section corresponds to an identifiable social unit within the building or complex. Position the largest and highest-spanning roofs over the most important communal spaces. Extend lesser roofs from these primary roof structures, and create the smallest roofs as half-vaults and sheds covering alcoves and thick walls.

name: FLOOR AND CEILING LAYOUT
problem: The fundamental challenge remains preserving the integrity of social spaces within the plan.
solution: Create a vault plan for each floor, primarily using two-way vaults, with one-way barrel vaults for spaces having length more than twice their width. Draw building sections while planning vaults, keeping these guidelines in mind: 1. Vaults should generally correspond to individual rooms. 2. Each vault's sides typically require support from a wall top, though beams or arches can occasionally substitute. 3. Vaults may span from 5 to 30 feet, but must have a rise at least 13 percent of their shorter span. 4. When one vault's edge extends more than a couple feet beyond the vault below it (in plan view), the lower vault must contain an arch to support the upper vault's load.

name: THICKENING THE OUTER WALLS
problem: THICK WALLS (197) establishes the importance of walls having "depth" and "volume" to accumulate character over time. However, this principle proves challenging to implement during building layout and construction.
solution: Identify all locations in your plan intended for seats and closets, as specified by patterns like ALCOVES (179), WINDOW PLACES (180), THICK WALLS (197), SUNNY COUNTER (199), WAIST-HIGH SHELF (201), and BUILT-IN SEATS (202). Mark these areas with a wide band on your plan, typically two to three feet deep. Recognize that these spaces will adjoin but remain distinct from the main room space - your seats, niches, and shelves will feel connected to rooms without being inside them. When positioning columns, arrange them to define these thick wall volumes as if they were small rooms or alcoves. For shallower elements like shelves and counters under 2 feet deep, simply deepen the columns and place shelves between them.

name: COLUMNS AT THE CORNERS
problem: We've established the principle that a building's structural components should align with its social spaces.
solution: On your preliminary building plan, mark dots representing columns at every room corner and at corners formed by smaller spaces like thick walls and alcoves. Then transfer these column locations to the actual site by placing stakes at the corresponding positions.

name: FINAL COLUMN DISTRIBUTION
problem: How should the spacing of wall-stiffening secondary columns vary according to ceiling height, number of stories, and room size?
solution: Position column stiffeners farthest apart on the ground floor, then progressively closer together as you move upward in the building. Specific column spacing depends on heights, loads, and wall thicknesses. While illustrative only, the following table shows approximate spacing needs: Stories Gnd floor 2ndfloor 3rdfloor 4thfloor 1 2' - 5' 2 3' - 6' 1' - 3' 3 4' - 8' 3' - 6' 1' - 3' 4 5' - 4' - 8' 3' - 6' 1' - 3' Mark these additional stiffening columns as dots between corner columns on your floor drawings. Space them evenly between corner column pairs, but ensure they're closer together along smaller room walls and farther apart along larger room walls.

name: ROOT FOUNDATIONS
problem: Trees demonstrate the ideal foundation model - their structure continues seamlessly below ground, creating a system completely integrated with the earth that functions in both tension and compression.
solution: Develop foundations where columns extend directly into the ground and spread outward there. The footing should be continuous with the column material so that the column and its footing, like a tree root, can resist not only compression but also tension and horizontal shear forces.

name: GROUND FLOOR SLAB
problem: When considering ground floor options, a slab provides the most cost-effective, straightforward, and natural foundation solution.
solution: Construct a ground floor slab that sits six to nine inches above ground level. Begin by constructing a short perimeter wall around the structure, connected to the column foundations. Then fill the enclosed area with a mixture of rubble, gravel, and concrete to create the raised slab.

name: BOX COLUMNS
problem: Throughout history and across traditional architecture worldwide, columns have served as expressive, beautiful, and treasured architectural elements. It is only in contemporary construction that they have lost their aesthetic significance and beauty.
solution: Design columns as filled hollow tubes with a rigid tubular exterior and a solid, compression-resistant core. Ensure the column's outer skin possesses some tensile strength—ideally within the skin material itself, though reinforcing wires in the filling material can also provide this strength.

name: PERIMETER BEAMS
problem: When constructing a room by first positioning columns at corners and then gradually developing walls and ceiling around them, the upper edge of the room requires a perimeter beam for structural integrity.
solution: Implement a continuous perimeter beam that encircles the room's upper edge. This beam should be robust enough to counteract the horizontal thrust from the vault above, distribute loads from upper floors onto the columns, bind the columns together, and serve as a lintel over wall openings. Ensure this beam forms a seamless connection with columns, walls, and the floor above, as well as with columns and walls below.

name: WALL MEMBRANES
problem: In organic building design, walls must contribute to the overall structural integrity. They need to work in harmony with all four adjoining structural elements while effectively resisting shear forces, bending moments, and compression loads.
solution: Construct the wall as a membrane that integrates seamlessly with columns, door frames, and window frames. For construction, first establish inner and outer membranes that can serve as finished surfaces, then pour the filling material between these membranes to complete the wall structure.

name: FLOOR-CEILING VAULTS
problem: We need a ceiling vault design that can support the live load of the floor above while forming the ceiling of the room below, all while minimizing bending and tension forces to allow reliance on compressive materials.
solution: Design floors and ceilings as elliptical vaults that rise between 13 and 20 percent of the shorter span. Utilize a construction method that allows the vault to be adapted to any room shape after walls and columns are already positioned. Importantly, avoid using prefabricated vaults under all circumstances.

name: ROOF VAULTS
problem: What roof shape provides optimal structural and aesthetic benefits?
solution: Construct the roof vault either as a cylindrical barrel vault or as a pitched roof with subtle convex curves along each sloping side. Incorporate undulations throughout the vault to enhance the shell's structural efficiency. Both the main shell's curvature and the undulations should be proportional to the span—larger spans require deeper curvature and more pronounced undulations.

name: NATURAL DOORS AND WINDOWS
problem: Determining the ideal placement for windows and doors requires nuanced consideration, yet few construction methods acknowledge this subtlety.
solution: Never use standardized doors or windows. Create windows of varying sizes based on their specific location. Don't determine the exact size or position of door and window frames until the room's rough framing is complete. Stand physically within the space and use your visual judgment to decide optimal placement and dimensions. Mark your chosen openings with string. As you progress higher in the building, make the windows progressively smaller.

name: LOW SILL
problem: A key function of windows is connecting occupants with the outdoor environment. Windows with excessively high sills create an undesirable separation from this connection.
solution: While determining window locations, identify which windows should feature lower sills. For first-floor windows intended for seated viewing, position sills between 11 and 14 inches from the floor. For windows on upper floors, raise the sill height to approximately 10 inches.

name: DEEP REVEALS
problem: Windows featuring sharp edges where the frame meets the wall create harsh glare and visual discomfort, making the adjoining rooms less pleasant to occupy.
solution: Design window frames with deep, splayed edges approximately one foot wide and angled at 50 to 60 degrees relative to the window plane. This gradual transition creates a smooth gradient between the bright light at the window and the darker inner wall surface, eliminating harsh contrasts.

higher_patterns.name: LIGHT ON TWO SIDES OF EVERY ROOM

name: LOW DOORWAY
problem: While tall doorways offer convenience and simplicity, lower doorways can create more meaningful spatial transitions and experiences.
solution: Rather than defaulting to standard 6' 8" rectangular door openings, design at least some doorways to be intentionally lower, transforming the act of passage into a deliberate, thoughtful transition between spaces. This approach is particularly effective for house entrances, private room entrances, and passages to intimate spaces like fire corners. Consider heights as low as 5' 8" for these transitional doorways.

name: FRAMES AS THICKENED EDGES
problem: A membrane with openings is vulnerable to tearing at those openings unless the edges are strengthened with additional material.
solution:
Rather than treating door and window frames as independent rigid structures inserted into wall openings, conceive of them as integral thickenings of the wall itself, designed to distribute the stress that concentrates around openings.
Following this concept, construct frames as extensions of the wall material, seamlessly integrated with the wall, using identical materials, and implemented in a way that maintains continuity with the wall's structure.

name: COLUMN PLACE
problem: Columns that are too slender or designed solely based on structural considerations will never create a comfortable environment.
solution: For freestanding columns, ensure they are at least as thick as a human body – minimum 12 inches, preferably 16 inches. Create spaces around them where people can comfortably sit or lean, such as a step, a small seat built against the column, or an area formed between paired columns.

name: COLUMN CONNECTION
problem: A structure's strength depends on its connection points; these junctions are most crucial at corners, particularly where columns join with beams.
solution: Create reinforced connections at column-beam junctions. Any material configuration that fills the corner space will work: fillets, gussets, column capitals, mushroom columns, or most universally, an arch that creates a continuous curve between column and beam.

name: STAIR VAULT
problem: In construction that maximizes compressive materials and minimizes wood usage, it makes sense to build stairs over vaulted spaces to conserve both weight and building materials.
solution: Construct a curved diagonal vault using the same techniques as your FLOOR-CEILING VAULTS (219). After the vault has hardened, cover it with lightweight concrete steps that are trowel-formed directly into position.

name: DUCT SPACE
problem: The location of utility pipes and conduits is often uncertain; they're hidden somewhere within walls, but their exact position remains unclear.
solution: Create dedicated ducts within the triangular space around the upper edge of each room, inside the vault, to house hot air conduits, plumbing, gas, and other utilities. Connect the ducts between different rooms using vertical chases in room corners. Install access panels and outlets at regular intervals along the duct to enable servicing of the conduits.

name: RADIANT HEAT
problem: This pattern provides a biologically accurate explanation for the intuitive understanding that sunlight and blazing fires deliver the most satisfying form of heat.
solution: Select a heating method for your spaces – particularly gathering rooms used during cold weather – that primarily relies on radiation rather than convection for heat transfer.

name: DORMER WINDOWS
problem: Based on our SHELTERING ROOF (117) discussion, we understand that a building's top story should be contained within the roof structure, enveloped by it.
solution: When installing windows in roof areas, construct dormer windows tall enough to stand in, and frame them as you would any other alcove in the building.

name: ROOF CAPS
problem: Throughout traditional architecture, builders have almost always incorporated some form of roof detail to provide an ornamental cap for the building.
solution: Select a roof capping method that harmonizes with both the construction style and the building's purpose. While these caps may serve structural functions, their primary role is decorative – they mark the summit and the point where the roof meets the sky.

name: FLOOR SURFACE
problem: Floors need to be comfortable, warm to touch, and inviting, while simultaneously being durable enough to withstand wear and maintain ease of cleaning.
solution: Divide your building into two distinct zone types: public areas and private/intimate spaces. For public zones, utilize hard materials such as waxed red polished concrete, tiles, or hardwood. In more intimate zones, create an underfloor of soft materials like felt, inexpensive nylon carpet, or straw matting, then layer it with cloths, pillows, carpets, and tapestries. Create a distinct boundary between these zones – perhaps incorporating a step – allowing people to remove their shoes when transitioning from public to intimate spaces.

name: LAPPED OUTSIDE WALLS
problem: The primary purpose of exterior walls is weather protection, which can only be achieved if materials are joined in ways that work together to create waterproof seams.
solution: Construct the exterior wall surface using weather-lapped materials: either "internally lapped" like exterior plaster, or literally overlapped, such as shingles, boards, and tiles. In either approach, select materials that can be easily and inexpensively repaired in small sections, allowing the wall to be indefinitely maintained in good condition through gradual upkeep.

name: SOFT INSIDE WALLS
problem: Interior walls that are excessively hard, cold, or solid are unpleasant to touch, prevent decoration, and produce hollow echoes.
solution: Ensure all interior surfaces are warm to touch, soft enough to accept small nails and tacks, and have a slight "give" when pressed. Soft plaster works excellently; textile hangings, canework, and weavings also provide this quality. Wood is also ideal where budget allows.

name: WINDOWS WHICH OPEN WIDE
problem: Many contemporary buildings lack opening windows entirely; and many existing opening windows fail to fulfill their intended purpose.
solution: Identify which windows should be operational. Select those that are easily accessible and positioned to capture pleasant scents from flowers, facilitate conversation with passersby, and harness natural breezes. Install outward-opening, side-hung casements for these locations. In select areas, consider installing full French windows for maximum openness.

name: SOLID DOORS WITH GLASS
problem: Fully opaque doors make sense in expansive houses or palaces where rooms are large enough to be self-contained environments, but in smaller buildings with compact rooms, completely solid doors rarely serve a useful purpose.
solution: Whenever possible, construct doors with glass elements, allowing visibility through at least the upper half. Simultaneously, build these doors with sufficient solidity to provide acoustic isolation and create a satisfying "thunk" sound when closed.

name: FILTERED LIGHT
problem: There's something captivating about light that passes through leaves or intricate patterns. What makes this filtered light so appealing?
solution: Create complex patterns of light and shadow where windows meet the sky or where roofs overhang. Develop a detailed interplay of brightness and darkness that diffuses harsh light and creates a gentler ambiance.

name: SMALL PANES
problem: With the invention of plate glass windows, it was assumed they would connect us more closely with nature. However, the effect has been quite the opposite.
solution: Break each window into multiple small panes. These should rarely exceed one foot square and can be much smaller. Determine appropriate pane dimensions by dividing the window's width and height by the desired number of panes. This approach creates uniquely proportioned panes for each window based on its specific dimensions.

name: HALF-INCH TRIM
problem: Buildings designed with machine-like precision don't require trim because of their exactness. However, this precision comes at a significant cost: it eliminates freedom and flexibility in building design.
solution: At every junction between different materials, install a piece of trim that covers the connection. Select trim pieces so that the narrowest section in each component is approximately 1/2 inch wide. Materials for trim can include wood, plaster, terracotta, and other similar options.

name: SEAT SPOTS
problem: Outdoor seating placed without consideration for views and climate conditions typically goes unused.
solution:
The location of outdoor seats matters far more than their design. When the spot is well-chosen, even the simplest bench works perfectly.
For cooler climates, position seats to capture sunlight and shield from wind; in warmer areas, place them in shaded locations with access to summer breezes. In all situations, orient seats to provide views of ongoing activities.

name: FRONT DOOR BENCH
problem: People enjoy observing street activities.
solution: Install a comfortable bench outside the front entrance where household members can sit comfortably for extended periods and observe passersby. Position the bench to establish a semi-private area in front of the home. Elements such as a low wall, plantings, or a tree can help define this transitional space.

name: SITTING WALL
problem: Walls and fences between outdoor areas are often too tall, yet having no boundary at all fails to acknowledge the nuanced distinctions between different spaces.
solution: Enclose natural outdoor spaces and create subtle boundaries between outdoor areas using low walls approximately 16 inches high. Make these walls wide enough to serve as seating—at least 12 inches across.

name: CANVAS ROOFS
problem: Tents and canvas awnings possess a unique beauty. Canvas has a pliability and gentleness that harmonizes with natural elements like wind, light, and sun. A structure incorporating canvas connects more intimately with these elements than one built solely with conventional rigid materials.
solution: Incorporate canvas roofs, walls, and awnings in spaces that would benefit from gentler light, partial summer shade, or protection from autumn and winter moisture. Design them to be retractable using ropes or wires, allowing for easy adjustment based on changing conditions.

name: RAISED FLOWERS
problem: Flowers enhance the borders of paths, buildings, and outdoor spaces, but these locations often expose them to foot traffic. Without protection, flowers in these areas struggle to thrive.
solution: Use flowers to create softer transitions at the edges of buildings, walkways, and outdoor areas. Elevate flower beds to make the blooms accessible for touching and smelling, and to provide convenient seating opportunities. Construct the flower bed edges solidly so people can sit among the flowers.

name: CLIMBING PLANTS
problem: A structure truly integrates with its environment when vegetation grows across portions of it as naturally as along the ground.
solution: On walls with good sun exposure, guide climbing plants to grow around the openings in the wall—windows, doors, porches, arcades, and trellises.

name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES
problem: While asphalt and concrete outdoor surfaces are easily maintained, they provide no benefits for pathways, rainwater management, or plant life.
solution: For paths and terraces, arrange paving stones with approximately 1-inch gaps between them to allow grass, moss, and small flowering plants to grow in these spaces. Place the stones directly on soil rather than mortar, and avoid using cement or mortar between the stones.

name: SOFT TILE AND BRICK
problem: How can someone feel connected to the earth, time, or their surroundings when walking on hard, industrial surfaces like concrete, asphalt, hard-fired architectural paving bricks, or artificial mixtures such as terrazzo?
solution:
Utilize bricks and tiles that are soft-baked and low-fired, allowing them to naturally wear over time and display signs of use.
These can be created on-site using simple molds with local clay. Surround the stacked clay pieces with twigs and firewood for firing, aiming for a soft pink color that will remain soft enough to develop a patina through use.

higher_patterns.name: PAVING WITH CRACKS BETWEEN THE STONES

name: ORNAMENT
problem: Humans naturally desire to embellish their surroundings.
solution:
Examine the building to identify edges and transitions that would benefit from emphasis or additional binding energy. Corners, material junctions, door frames, windows, entrances, wall intersections, garden gates, and fences all naturally invite ornamentation.
Develop simple themes and apply elements of these themes repeatedly to the edges and boundaries you've selected for emphasis. Design ornaments to function as seams along these boundaries, connecting the two sides and unifying them.

name: WARM COLORS
problem: The greenish and grayish tones common in hospitals and office hallways create a cold, depressing atmosphere. Natural wood, sunlight, and vibrant colors provide warmth. The color temperature in a room significantly affects comfort levels.
solution: Select surface colors that work with natural light, reflected light, and artificial lighting to generate a warm atmosphere throughout the rooms.

name: DIFFERENT CHAIRS
problem: People have different body sizes and sitting preferences, yet modern design often produces uniformly identical chairs.
solution: Avoid furnishing any space with identical chairs. Instead, select a diverse collection of seating options: varying in size (some large, some small), comfort level (some softer than others), style (some rockers), age (some antique, some contemporary), features (some with arms, some without), and materials (wicker, wood, upholstered).

name: POOLS OF LIGHT
problem: Even lighting—the preferred approach of lighting engineers—serves no practical purpose. In fact, it undermines the social character of spaces and creates feelings of disorientation and boundlessness.
solution: Install lights at low heights and spaced apart to create distinct pools of illumination that encompass furniture arrangements like protective bubbles, enhancing the social nature of these spaces. Remember that these pools of light require darker areas between them to be effective.

name: THINGS FROM YOUR LIFE
problem: The concepts of "decor" and "interior design" have become so widespread that people often ignore their natural instincts about which items they truly want to surround themselves with.
solution: Don't be misled into thinking modern decor must follow current trends—whether sleek, psychedelic, "natural," "modern art," plant-focused, or whatever else fashion dictates. The most beautiful surroundings emerge directly from your life—objects you genuinely care about and items that tell your personal story.
